content.text: If you owe money to the IRS and cannot pay it all at once, you are not alone. Millions of Americans face tax debt every year, and the good news is that the IRS offers multiple programs designed to help people just like you resolve what they owe. Tax relief is the umbrella term for these programs, and understanding your options is the first step to financial freedom.
What Is Tax Relief?

Tax relief refers to any legal method that reduces, delays, or eliminates the amount of taxes you owe. The IRS and state tax agencies have specific programs in place for taxpayers who are unable to pay their full tax liability. These are official pathways designed to help struggling taxpayers get back on track.
Common Types of Tax Relief Programs

items: Offer in Compromise (OIC) — settle your debt for less than you owe
Installment Agreements — pay your balance over time in monthly payments
Currently Not Collectible (CNC) Status — temporarily pause IRS collection
Innocent Spouse Relief — protection if your spouse caused the tax debt
Penalty Abatement — reduce or eliminate IRS penalties
Bankruptcy — discharge certain tax debts in extreme cases

content.text: Who Qualifies for Tax Relief?

Qualification depends on the specific program. For an Offer in Compromise, the IRS evaluates your income, expenses, assets, and overall ability to pay. For installment agreements, almost any taxpayer who owes less than $50,000 can qualify for a streamlined plan. Penalty abatement is often available to first-time offenders who have otherwise been compliant.
Why You Should Not Face the IRS Alone

Navigating IRS tax programs is complex and intimidating. IRS agents are trained negotiators, and without professional representation, you may end up agreeing to a payment plan that is impossible to keep. A tax relief professional knows which programs you qualify for and how to present your case in the best possible light. At SympleTax, our licensed professionals handle everything from the first IRS contact to the final resolution. Do not wait until the IRS takes action. Schedule a free consultation with SympleTax and find out exactly which programs you qualify for. Wage garnishments, bank levies, and tax liens can happen quickly. content.text: Imagine owing the IRS $80,000 and settling that debt for $3,200. That is exactly what the Offer in Compromise (OIC) program can do. It is one of the most powerful tax relief tools available, and SympleTax helps clients leverage it every single day.
What Is an Offer in Compromise?

An Offer in Compromise is an agreement between a taxpayer and the IRS that settles a tax liability for less than the full amount owed. The IRS accepts an OIC when it believes collecting the full amount is unlikely or would create economic hardship.
How Does the IRS Evaluate an OIC?

The IRS uses a formula called Reasonable Collection Potential (RCP) to determine what they can realistically collect from you. This calculation factors in your monthly disposable income, the value of your assets, allowable living expenses based on national and local standards, and your future income potential. If your RCP is significantly lower than what you owe, you may be an excellent candidate for an OIC.
Three Types of OIC

items: Doubt as to Collectibility — you cannot afford to pay the full amount
Doubt as to Liability — there is a legitimate dispute about whether you owe the amount
Effective Tax Administration — paying would cause unfair economic hardship

content.text: OIC Application Process

Filing an Offer in Compromise requires submitting Form 656 along with detailed financial documentation. The IRS has up to 24 months to evaluate your offer. During this time, collection activity is generally suspended. Getting the offer amount right is critical — too low and the IRS rejects it; too high and you overpay.
Success Rates and Common Mistakes

The IRS accepts roughly 40% of OIC applications. The most common reasons for rejection include incomplete financial disclosure, unrealistic offer amounts, and failure to stay compliant with current filings. content.text: If you owe back taxes but cannot pay the full amount right now, an IRS Installment Agreement may be the lifeline you need. Rather than facing liens, levies, or wage garnishments, you can set up a manageable monthly payment plan and stay in good standing with the IRS.
What Is an IRS Installment Agreement?

An Installment Agreement is a formal arrangement between you and the IRS to pay your tax debt over time through monthly payments. Once approved, the IRS typically suspends most collection activity as long as you remain current.
Types of Installment Agreements

items: Guaranteed Installment Agreement — for balances up to $10,000; the IRS must accept it
Streamlined Installment Agreement — for balances up to $50,000; no financial statement required
Non-Streamlined Agreement — for balances over $50,000; requires full financial disclosure
Partial Pay Installment Agreement (PPIA) — you pay less than the full amount over time

content.text: How Penalties and Interest Work During a Payment Plan

Interest and penalties continue to accrue during an Installment Agreement. The IRS charges interest at the federal short-term rate plus 3%, plus a failure-to-pay penalty of 0.25% per month during an approved agreement. This is why paying more than the minimum — or exploring an OIC first — can save you significant money.
Setting Up Your Plan Correctly

Many taxpayers make the mistake of agreeing to a payment amount they cannot sustain. If you miss payments, the agreement can default and you will be back where you started — except with more interest accrued. SympleTax works with you to set a realistic payment amount. Schedule a consultation today to find out whether an installment agreement or another program serves you better. In many cases, clients save thousands by exploring all options first. Reach out to SympleTax and let us evaluate your full situation before you commit to a payment plan.

content.text: When your financial situation is genuinely dire, the IRS has a program that puts collection of your tax debt on hold. It is called Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status, and it can provide critical breathing room for taxpayers in financial crisis.
What Is Currently Not Collectible Status?

CNC status is a designation the IRS assigns when collecting the debt would leave you unable to pay basic living expenses. When active, the IRS suspends all collection activity — no wage garnishments, no bank levies, no collection calls.
Who Qualifies for CNC?

To qualify, your allowable living expenses must equal or exceed your monthly income. The IRS uses national and local expense standards to determine what counts as a necessary expense, including housing, utilities, food, transportation, health insurance, and minimum payments on secured debts.
What Happens While in CNC Status?

The IRS reviews your financial situation periodically to determine if your circumstances have changed. The 10-year statute of limitations on IRS collections continues to run while you are in CNC — meaning the clock keeps ticking toward your debt potentially expiring.
Is CNC a Permanent Solution?

Not always. If your income increases, the IRS may remove the CNC designation and resume collection. However, for clients on fixed incomes or facing long-term disability, CNC status can last for years or until the debt expires entirely. CNC is especially powerful when combined with other strategies. title: IRS Penalty Abatement: How to Get Your Penalties Removed
excerpt: IRS penalties can sometimes make up 25% or more of your total tax bill. The good news is that the IRS will often reduce or completely eliminate those penalties if you know how to ask. This process is called Penalty Abatement, and it is one of the most underutilized forms of tax…

content.text: IRS penalties can sometimes make up 25% or more of your total tax bill. The good news is that the IRS will often reduce or completely eliminate those penalties if you know how to ask. This process is called Penalty Abatement, and it is one of the most underutilized forms of tax relief available.
Types of IRS Penalties

items: Failure to File — 5% per month, up to 25% of unpaid taxes
Failure to Pay — 0.5% per month, up to 25% of unpaid taxes
Accuracy-Related — 20% of underpaid taxes due to negligence
Fraud — 75% of the underpayment (rare but severe)

content.text: First-Time Penalty Abatement

The IRS First-Time Abatement (FTA) policy is the easiest route to penalty relief. You qualify if you have a clean penalty history for the three tax years prior to the year in question, have filed all required returns, and have paid or arranged to pay any tax due. FTA can be requested by phone and is often granted within minutes of a call.
Reasonable Cause Abatement

If you do not qualify for FTA, you may still qualify under Reasonable Cause. The IRS removes penalties if you can show that failure to comply was due to circumstances beyond your control, such as serious illness, death of a family member, natural disaster, or reliance on incorrect IRS advice.
How to Request Penalty Abatement

You can request abatement by phone, by mail using Form 843, or through your tax professional. How you frame your case matters enormously. A well-crafted letter citing specific IRS policy dramatically improves your chances. SympleTax has helped clients save tens of thousands of dollars in penalties alone. title: Innocent Spouse Relief: You Should Not Pay for Your Spouse's Tax Mistakes
excerpt: When you file a joint tax return, you and your spouse become equally responsible for everything on that return — including mistakes, omissions, or fraud. But what happens when you had no idea your spouse was hiding income or claiming false deductions? That is where Innocent…

content.text: When you file a joint tax return, you and your spouse become equally responsible for everything on that return — including mistakes, omissions, or fraud. But what happens when you had no idea your spouse was hiding income or claiming false deductions? That is where Innocent Spouse Relief comes in.
What Is Innocent Spouse Relief?

Innocent Spouse Relief is an IRS program that releases one spouse from joint tax liability when the other spouse created the debt through error or fraud, without the innocent spouse's knowledge. It is a legal protection for people who find themselves on the hook for a tax debt they did not cause.
Three Types of Innocent Spouse Relief

items: Classic Innocent Spouse Relief (IRC 6015(b)) — for erroneous items on a joint return where you had no knowledge
Separation of Liability Relief (IRC 6015(c)) — allocates the debt between spouses based on who was responsible
Equitable Relief (IRC 6015(f)) — a catch-all for situations that do not fit the other two but where holding you liable would be unfair

content.text: Who Qualifies?

To qualify, you must show that you filed a joint return, there was an understatement of tax due to your spouse's erroneous items, you did not know and had no reason to know of the understatement when you signed, and it would be unfair to hold you liable.
Time Limits and Divorce

For certain forms of relief, you must request it within two years of the IRS beginning collection activity. Even if your divorce decree states your ex-spouse is responsible for the taxes, the IRS is not bound by that agreement and can still pursue you. Only an approved Innocent Spouse Relief request provides true protection. If you are being held responsible for your spouse's tax mistakes, contact SympleTax — we will evaluate your eligibility and fight to protect you. Do not accept liability that is not yours. Speak with a SympleTax specialist today about your Innocent Spouse options.

content.text: Few things are as damaging to your financial life as a federal tax lien. It can destroy your credit, prevent you from selling your home, and make it nearly impossible to get a loan. Here is what you need to know — and what you can do about it.
What Is a Federal Tax Lien?

A federal tax lien is the government's legal claim against all of your property when you fail to pay a tax debt after demand. The lien attaches to your real estate, personal property, and financial assets. It is different from a tax levy — a lien is the legal claim, while a levy is the actual seizure of property.
How Tax Liens Affect You

items: Severely damages your credit standing and public record
Blocks refinancing or selling your home without first satisfying the lien
Attaches to future assets you acquire
Can affect your ability to obtain business licenses or commercial financing

content.text: How to Get a Tax Lien Released

The most straightforward way to get a lien released is to pay your tax debt in full. Once paid, the IRS must release the lien within 30 days. But full payment is not the only option. A discharge removes the lien from a specific property so you can sell it. Subordination allows another creditor to take priority so you can get financing. Withdrawal removes the public notice of the lien even if debt remains, in certain circumstances. An approved Offer in Compromise also results in a lien release upon completion.
The Fresh Start Initiative and Liens

The IRS Fresh Start Initiative raised the minimum debt required to file a lien and made it easier to withdraw liens after entering a direct debit installment agreement in certain conditions. A tax lien does not have to follow you forever. Work with SympleTax to develop a strategy that removes or minimizes the impact of your lien. Ready to protect your credit and financial future? content.text: A tax levy is one of the most aggressive actions the IRS can take — and it can happen faster than most people expect. Understanding what a levy is, how it happens, and how to stop it could save your paycheck, your bank account, and your peace of mind.
What Is an IRS Tax Levy?

A tax levy is the IRS's legal seizure of your property to satisfy a tax debt. Unlike a lien (which is just a legal claim), a levy actually takes the property. The IRS can levy wages, bank accounts, retirement accounts, Social Security benefits, real estate, and vehicles.
Types of IRS Levies

items: Wage Garnishment — a continuous levy where your employer sends a portion of every paycheck to the IRS
Bank Levy — a one-time seizure of funds in your bank account (held for 21 days before transfer)
Social Security Levy — up to 15% of your monthly benefit
Property Seizure — the IRS can seize and sell real estate and vehicles

content.text: The 21-Day Bank Levy Window

When your bank account is levied, the bank must hold the funds for 21 days before releasing them to the IRS. This window exists specifically to give you time to act. Acting immediately may allow you to stop the levy and recover your money.
How to Stop a Levy

Options include paying the debt in full, entering an Installment Agreement, submitting an Offer in Compromise, requesting Currently Not Collectible status, or requesting a Collection Due Process hearing. Once you take qualifying action, the IRS must release the levy. SympleTax specializes in emergency levy releases. Contact us immediately if the IRS has levied your wages or bank account — every hour counts. Do not wait for the situation to get worse. Reach out to SympleTax today and let us stop the levy and build a resolution plan.

content.text: The IRS Fresh Start Initiative was created to give struggling taxpayers a more accessible path to resolving their tax debt. Since its launch in 2011 and subsequent expansions, it has helped millions of Americans get out from under crippling tax burdens.
What Is the IRS Fresh Start Program?

Fresh Start is not a single program but a collection of policy changes that make it easier for individuals and small businesses to resolve tax debt. Key changes include more flexible Offer in Compromise terms, higher thresholds for tax liens, expanded installment agreement eligibility, and easier access to penalty abatement.
Offer in Compromise Changes Under Fresh Start

Fresh Start significantly expanded OIC eligibility by changing how the IRS calculates reasonable collection potential. The look-forward period for future income was reduced from 60 or 48 months to 12 or 24 months, depending on your offer terms. This means settlement amounts are often much lower than before Fresh Start.
Installment Agreement Expansion

Under Fresh Start, streamlined installment agreements are now available for tax debts up to $50,000, and the repayment period was extended to 72 months. More people can now qualify without providing a full financial statement.
Who Is Fresh Start For?

Fresh Start is designed for taxpayers who have the willingness to comply but lack the financial means to pay in full. Self-employed individuals, small business owners, and those who have experienced financial hardship are exactly who this program was built for. Fresh Start has changed the game for tax debt resolution. Talk to SympleTax about your Fresh Start options and begin the process today. Your fresh start is one call away. content.text: Being self-employed is rewarding but it comes with tax responsibilities that catch many freelancers, contractors, and business owners off guard. If you have fallen behind on self-employment taxes, you are not alone — and real solutions are available.
Why Self-Employed Taxpayers Accumulate More Debt

When you are self-employed, no employer withholds taxes from your paycheck. You are responsible for making quarterly estimated tax payments to cover federal income tax and self-employment tax of 15.3%. Many self-employed individuals skip these payments during slow periods and find themselves with a large bill — plus penalties.
Common Tax Problems for the Self-Employed

items: Failure to pay quarterly estimated taxes
Underreporting income from multiple 1099 clients
Mixing business and personal expenses incorrectly
Failing to file returns for multiple years
Not accounting for self-employment tax in addition to income tax

content.text: Unfiled Returns: The Danger of Multiple Missing Years

The IRS can file a Substitute for Return on your behalf — without your deductions, credits, or business expenses. This almost always results in a higher tax bill than you would actually owe if you filed correctly. Getting those returns filed properly is often the first step in resolution.
Solutions Available to Self-Employed Taxpayers

Self-employed individuals have access to all the same IRS relief programs as W-2 employees, including Offer in Compromise, Installment Agreements, Penalty Abatement, and Currently Not Collectible status. In many cases, properly amending or filing past returns reduces the balance dramatically before any relief program is even applied. SympleTax has helped hundreds of self-employed taxpayers resolve years of back taxes. Schedule a free consultation here — the solution is usually more accessible than you think. content.text: Most people think of the IRS when they hear tax debt — but state tax agencies can be just as aggressive in collecting what they are owed. Understanding the difference between state and federal tax debt is critical to building the right resolution strategy.
How Federal and State Tax Debt Differ

Federal tax debt is owed to the IRS, a federal agency with nationwide jurisdiction. State tax debt is owed to your state's department of revenue. While both can garnish wages, file liens, and levy accounts, state agencies often move faster and have fewer formal procedural requirements than the IRS.
State Tax Agencies Can Be More Aggressive

Unlike the IRS, which must follow strict procedures before taking collection action, many state tax agencies have fewer restrictions. Some states can garnish wages with little or no warning. California's FTB, New York's DTF, and similar agencies are known for aggressive collection practices — yet each state also has its own relief programs.
Resolution Options for State Tax Debt

items: State Installment Agreements — similar to IRS payment plans but governed by state rules
State Offer in Compromise — not all states offer this, but many do
Penalty Waiver Programs — most states offer penalty reduction for hardship cases
Hardship designations similar to Currently Not Collectible

content.text: Dealing with Both Federal and State Debt Simultaneously

Many clients come to SympleTax with debt owed to both the IRS and their state. Coordinating resolution across both agencies requires careful strategy — settling federal debt first can change your financial picture in ways that affect state negotiations. Whether you owe the IRS, your state, or both, SympleTax handles it all — reach out for a free consultation and we will map out a complete resolution plan. Do not let state tax debt sneak up on you. title: What Happens If You Do Not File Your Tax Return?
excerpt: Life gets complicated. Whether it was financial hardship, a personal crisis, or simple avoidance, millions of Americans have one or more years of unfiled tax returns. What happens if you just do not file? The answer may surprise you — and it is important to know before the IRS…

content.text: Life gets complicated. Whether it was financial hardship, a personal crisis, or simple avoidance, millions of Americans have one or more years of unfiled tax returns. What happens if you just do not file? The answer may surprise you — and it is important to know before the IRS comes looking.
The IRS Will Eventually Find You

The IRS receives copies of all W-2s and 1099s issued to you by employers and clients. Even if you do not file, they know roughly what you earned. They can and will file a Substitute for Return on your behalf — using only the income documents they have, with no deductions, exemptions, or credits you might have claimed.
What Is a Substitute for Return?

An SFR is a return the IRS prepares when you fail to file. It uses the most basic filing status with the standard deduction only and ignores business expenses, dependents, retirement contributions, and other deductions. The result is almost always a much higher tax bill than you would have owed with a properly filed return.
Criminal Charges Are Possible

While rare, the IRS can prosecute individuals for willful failure to file. Criminal charges can result in fines and imprisonment. The threat is most real for those who continue to ignore their filing obligations year after year with clear knowledge of their obligation.
The Statute of Limitations Does Not Run on Unfiled Returns
Normally the IRS has three years from when a return is filed to assess taxes. If you never file, that clock never starts — the IRS can assess taxes at any time, with no expiration.
How to Fix Unfiled Returns

The solution is to file the returns. In most cases, penalties for late filing are manageable, especially if penalty abatement applies. Filing often dramatically reduces the balance compared to an IRS-prepared Substitute for Return. If you have unfiled returns, the worst thing you can do is nothing. title: The IRS Collection Process: A Timeline of What to Expect
excerpt: Understanding how the IRS collections process works — step by step — helps you know exactly where you stand and when you need to act. Ignoring IRS notices does not make them go away. The process escalates predictably, and each step reduces your available options. After the IRS…
author: SympleTax Team
metaTitle: The IRS Collection Process: A Timeline of What to Expect | SympleTax
metaDescription: Understanding how the IRS collections process works — step by step — helps you know exactly where you stand and when you need to act. Ignoring IRS notices doe…

content.text: Understanding how the IRS collections process works — step by step — helps you know exactly where you stand and when you need to act. Ignoring IRS notices does not make them go away. The process escalates predictably, and each step reduces your available options.
Step 1: Notice CP14 — Balance Due

After the IRS determines you owe taxes, they send Notice CP14 — a balance due notice. You have 30 days to respond before escalation begins. At this stage, you still have maximum options for resolution.
Step 2: Reminder Notices
If you do not respond to CP14, the IRS sends follow-up notices — CP501, CP502, and CP503. Each is progressively more urgent. Interest and penalties continue accruing throughout.
Step 3: Notice CP504 — Intent to Levy
CP504 informs you that the IRS intends to levy your state tax refund and is beginning the broader levy process. The urgency has significantly increased at this point.
Step 4: Final Notice — LT11 or Letter 1058

This is the critical notice. The Final Notice of Intent to Levy gives you 30 days to request a Collection Due Process hearing, which stops most IRS collection action while your case is reviewed. Missing this deadline can be extremely costly.
Step 5: Active Collection — Levies and Liens
After the CDP period expires, the IRS can file tax liens and issue levies. This is when bank accounts get frozen and wages get garnished.
Step 6: Collections Statute Expiration

The IRS generally has 10 years from the date of assessment to collect. After this date, the debt legally expires. Strategic use of this statute is one of the many tools in SympleTax's approach. Wherever you are in this process, SympleTax can help — act now while you still have the most options available to you. Do not wait for the next notice. Schedule a consultation with SympleTax and we will take it from here.

content.text: Your tax debt does not just affect your relationship with the IRS — it can ripple through your entire financial life. Understanding exactly how tax debt impacts your credit and what you can do about it is essential for anyone dealing with IRS issues.
Federal Tax Liens and Credit Reports

Prior to 2018, federal tax liens appeared directly on credit reports and could devastate scores. A major change occurred when the three major credit bureaus agreed to remove tax liens from consumer credit reports. This means most federal tax liens no longer directly appear on your credit report — but their consequences still affect your financial life.
Indirect Credit Impacts Still Exist

If you have a lien on your property, you may be unable to refinance or sell real estate, limiting your liquidity and financial flexibility. Lenders who perform thorough due diligence — particularly for mortgages or business loans — may discover a tax lien through public records searches even if it does not appear on your credit report.
Resolving Your Tax Debt Protects Your Financial Future

The best way to protect your financial health is to resolve your tax debt. Once an Offer in Compromise is completed or an Installment Agreement is fully paid, the IRS releases the lien within 30 days and the public record is cleared. Some programs even allow lien withdrawal while you are still in a payment arrangement. Do not let tax debt hold back your financial future. content.text: Running a small business is hard enough without the weight of tax debt hanging over you. Whether it is unpaid payroll taxes, corporate income taxes, or sales taxes, business tax debt can threaten not just your company but your personal finances as well.
The Most Dangerous Type of Business Tax Debt: Payroll Taxes

If your business has employees and you have been withholding federal payroll taxes but not remitting them to the IRS, you are carrying significant risk. The IRS takes trust fund taxes extremely seriously because those withheld funds legally belong to your employees, not to the business. The Trust Fund Recovery Penalty (TFRP) allows the IRS to hold individual business owners, officers, and even bookkeepers personally liable for 100% of the unpaid trust fund taxes. This debt survives bankruptcy and follows you personally even if the business closes.
Payroll Tax Resolution Options

items: Installment Agreements for business tax debt
Offer in Compromise for business liabilities
Trust Fund Recovery Penalty defense — disputing personal liability
Business closure and proper wind-down procedures

content.text: Protecting Personal Assets from Business Tax Debt

One of the most important things a business owner can do is understand the boundary between business and personal liability. Corporations and LLCs typically protect personal assets, but the TFRP can pierce that protection. Working with a tax professional early helps protect what you have built. content.text: Many retirees believe their Social Security benefits are safe from the IRS. Unfortunately, that is not entirely true. While certain protections exist, the IRS can and does garnish Social Security income in specific circumstances.
Can the IRS Garnish Social Security?

Yes. Under the Federal Payment Levy Program, the IRS can levy up to 15% of your monthly Social Security benefit to satisfy a tax debt. This happens automatically and does not require a court order. It can apply to both retirement benefits and disability benefits.
Who Is Most at Risk?

Retirees and disabled individuals who owe federal income taxes are most at risk. Common situations include failure to withhold from retirement account distributions, not paying taxes on Social Security benefits above income thresholds, or carrying pre-retirement tax debt into retirement.
Protections Available to Seniors

Seniors on fixed incomes often have strong cases for Currently Not Collectible status or Offer in Compromise. The IRS's own allowable expense standards often consume most or all of a retiree's Social Security income, making collection impractical. This can result in CNC status or a very low OIC settlement. content.text: Divorce is already one of life's most stressful events. Add tax debt to the mix and things get complicated fast. Joint tax returns mean joint liability, and that liability does not automatically go away when a marriage ends.
Joint and Several Liability

When you file a joint return, both spouses are jointly and severally liable for the entire tax debt. This means the IRS can pursue either of you for 100% of the balance — regardless of what your divorce decree says about who is responsible.
Your Divorce Decree Does Not Bind the IRS

Even if your divorce agreement clearly states that your ex-spouse will pay all tax debts, the IRS is not a party to that agreement. If your ex does not pay, the IRS will come after you. Your recourse against your ex is through the civil courts — not by arguing with the IRS.
Options for Protection

items: Innocent Spouse Relief — if your ex created the debt through fraud or error without your knowledge
Separation of Liability Relief — allocates the debt based on each person's responsibility
Equitable Relief — a flexible catch-all where full liability would be unfair

content.text: Proactive Steps During Divorce Negotiations

Before finalizing a divorce, address any potential tax issues. Ensure all joint returns have been filed. If there is existing tax debt, consider resolving it before the divorce is finalized so both parties have a clean slate. Navigating tax debt through or after divorce requires specialized expertise. SympleTax protects your rights — contact us for a free consultation. One relationship ending should not cost you everything. Talk to SympleTax today about your options.

content.text: Receiving an IRS notice is enough to make anyone's heart race. But most IRS notices are informational or request simple responses. Understanding what each notice means — and what action is required — can save you significant stress and money.
CP14 — Balance Due

This is typically the first notice you receive when you owe taxes. It shows the amount due and requests payment within 21 days. At this stage, your options are most flexible — you can pay, request an installment agreement, or begin the Offer in Compromise process.
CP2000 — Underreporter Notice

This notice means the IRS has received income information that does not match your return. It proposes additional tax, interest, and penalties. This is not an audit — it is a proposed adjustment. You have the right to agree, dispute, or provide additional documentation.
CP504 — Intent to Levy
This notice is serious. It informs you that the IRS intends to seize your state tax refund and warns that other property may be seized. Respond immediately — do not set this aside.
LT11 / Letter 1058 — Final Notice of Intent to Levy

This is your last chance before active levy begins. You have 30 days to request a Collection Due Process hearing, which temporarily stops IRS collection action. Missing this 30-day window can be extremely costly.
CP90 / CP297 — Notice of Levy

This notice means the IRS has already served a levy on your wages or bank account. Immediate action is required. Got an IRS notice and not sure what to do? content.text: What You Need to Know

Many people are surprised to learn that certain tax debts can be discharged in bankruptcy. It is not automatic, and the rules are strict — but for the right client, bankruptcy can eliminate federal and state income tax debt permanently.
The 3-2-240 Rule

For federal income taxes to be dischargeable in Chapter 7, all of the following must be true: the tax return was due at least three years ago (including extensions), the return was filed at least two years ago, and the tax was assessed at least 240 days ago. The return must not be fraudulent and you must not have been guilty of tax evasion.
What Cannot Be Discharged

Payroll taxes, tax liens that attached before bankruptcy, taxes related to fraudulent returns, and recently assessed taxes generally cannot be discharged. Bankruptcy is not a universal solution for all tax debts.
Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Tax Debt

Chapter 7 can eliminate qualifying income tax debt entirely. Chapter 13 allows you to pay non-dischargeable taxes over a 3-5 year repayment plan, potentially stopping collection action and interest accrual on certain debts.
When IRS Programs Are Better

For most clients, IRS resolution programs like OIC or Currently Not Collectible status are preferable to bankruptcy. Bankruptcy has significant credit consequences. However, for clients with very old tax debts and no assets, it can be the optimal strategy. SympleTax evaluates all options before recommending a path. Schedule a free consultation and we will determine whether bankruptcy or an IRS program is right for you. Your situation is unique and deserves a tailored strategy. content.text: Who Can Represent You Before the IRS?
Not everyone can represent you before the IRS. Only three types of professionals have unlimited practice rights: Enrolled Agents, Certified Public Accountants, and Tax Attorneys.
What Is an Enrolled Agent?

An Enrolled Agent is a federally licensed tax professional who has either passed a rigorous three-part IRS examination or worked for the IRS for five or more years. EAs must complete 72 hours of continuing education every three years and are governed by IRS ethical standards.
EA vs. CPA vs. Tax Attorney

CPAs are experts in accounting and financial reporting; not all specialize in tax controversy. Tax attorneys are ideal for complex litigation. Enrolled Agents are uniquely focused on tax compliance and IRS representation — making them specialists for exactly the work tax debt resolution requires.
Why SympleTax Uses Licensed Professionals

Your representation is backed by professional credentials, ethical obligations, and deep IRS expertise — not a call center script. When choosing tax representation, credentials matter enormously. content.text: The IRS Collection Deadline You May Not Know About

The IRS does not have forever to collect your tax debt. There is a 10-year clock called the Collection Statute Expiration Date (CSED) that, when it runs out, legally extinguishes your federal tax debt.
How the CSED Is Calculated

The 10-year period begins on the date the IRS officially assesses the tax — usually the date your return is processed or an audit is finalized. Knowing your specific CSED dates is essential to any resolution strategy.
Actions That Pause the Clock

The CSED can be paused by filing for bankruptcy (plus six months after discharge), submitting an Offer in Compromise (during evaluation plus 30 days), requesting a CDP hearing, and living outside the US for six or more months. These pauses extend how long the IRS has to collect.
Strategic Implications

For clients with older tax debts, the CSED can be a powerful tool. If the debt is close to expiring, a strategy like Currently Not Collectible status can allow the clock to run out without making large payments. SympleTax always analyzes the CSED for every client.
Important Warning

Never file for bankruptcy, submit an OIC, or sign an installment agreement extension without understanding how it affects your CSED. Well-meaning actions can inadvertently extend the IRS collection window by years. Understanding your CSED dates can change your entire strategy. Contact SympleTax and we will pull your full account history and calculate every relevant date. The clock may already be working in your favor. Find out where you stand with a free SympleTax review.

content.text: The IRS and Crypto: Everything Is Taxable

The IRS treats cryptocurrency as property, not currency. Every sale, trade, exchange, or use of crypto to purchase goods or services is a taxable event that must be reported. Many crypto investors were caught off guard when the IRS began enforcing crypto tax compliance.
What Triggers Crypto Tax Liability

You owe taxes when you sell crypto for cash, trade one cryptocurrency for another, use crypto to buy goods or services, receive crypto as payment for work (ordinary income), and receive staking or mining rewards.
IRS Crypto Enforcement

The IRS has been increasingly aggressive about crypto tax enforcement. Major exchanges now issue 1099 forms and share data with the IRS. If you had crypto gains you did not report, the IRS likely already knows about them.
Resolving Unreported Crypto Income

The first step is to reconstruct your transaction history and determine your actual tax liability. In many cases, proper cost basis accounting significantly reduces what you actually owe. If you owe back taxes on crypto, all standard IRS resolution programs apply.
Voluntary Disclosure Programs

For taxpayers with significant unreported crypto income, the IRS Voluntary Disclosure Program allows proactive resolution before the IRS contacts you — usually with reduced penalties. If you have unreported crypto income, acting proactively is always better than waiting. Talk to SympleTax about your options before the IRS reaches out first. Crypto tax issues are resolvable. content.text: The Gig Economy Tax Trap

The gig economy has created flexibility for millions of workers — but it has also created a tax trap that catches drivers, delivery workers, and freelancers every year. When platforms pay you, they do not withhold taxes. That responsibility falls entirely on you.
How Much Gig Workers Actually Owe

As a gig worker, you owe federal income tax on your net earnings plus self-employment tax of 15.3%. On $40,000 of net gig income, your total federal and state tax bill could easily exceed $12,000 — money that has to come from your own pocket if you did not set it aside.
Quarterly Estimated Payments

Gig workers are required to pay quarterly estimated taxes. If you skip these, you will owe penalties on top of the underlying tax. The IRS safe harbor rule says you avoid underpayment penalties if you pay at least 100% of last year's tax liability.
Deductions That Can Reduce Your Bill

Gig workers can deduct mileage, phone and data plans (business portion), supplies and equipment, and a portion of health insurance premiums. Proper tracking of these deductions can dramatically reduce your taxable income.
What to Do If You Already Owe

If you have accumulated gig economy tax debt, all standard IRS relief programs apply. SympleTax has helped dozens of gig workers resolve back taxes — often by first amending returns to capture missed deductions. Gig income created a tax problem that has real solutions. content.text: What Is IRS Wage Garnishment?

IRS wage garnishment — technically a continuous levy — is when the IRS instructs your employer to withhold a portion of every paycheck and send it directly to the IRS. It continues automatically until the debt is resolved or an alternative arrangement is made.
How Much Can the IRS Take?

Your employer calculates your exempt amount based on your standard deduction and personal exemptions. Everything above that goes to the IRS. For many people, 50 to 70% of their take-home pay is redirected, making basic living expenses impossible to cover.
How to Stop Wage Garnishment

The IRS is required to release a wage garnishment once you pay the debt in full, enter an installment agreement, have an Offer in Compromise pending, demonstrate financial hardship and receive a Currently Not Collectible designation, or when the IRS made a procedural error in the levy process.
The Fastest Route to Release

The fastest way to stop a garnishment is to call the IRS with a proposal through a licensed tax professional who can negotiate immediately. SympleTax can often get a wage garnishment released within days of being hired.
Prevention Is Better

If you have received a Final Notice of Intent to Levy, do not wait for the garnishment to start. Acting before the levy begins gives us more leverage and more options to find the right resolution. content.text: When Life Events Create Tax Debt

A serious illness, a job loss, a death in the family — these are not just personal tragedies. They are events that can derail your finances and create tax debt that feels impossible to face. The IRS has specific programs designed for taxpayers experiencing genuine hardship.
Hardship as a Qualifying Factor

The IRS recognizes that life circumstances matter. Hardship is a qualifying factor for Currently Not Collectible status, Penalty Abatement under Reasonable Cause, Offer in Compromise under Effective Tax Administration, and installment agreement modifications.
Medical Bills and Allowable Expenses

When the IRS evaluates your financial situation, they use national and local standards for allowable expenses. However, if you have ongoing medical expenses that exceed these standards, you can submit documentation to claim higher allowable expenses — reducing what the IRS believes you can afford to pay.
Documenting Your Hardship

Effective hardship claims are specific and well-documented. Gather medical records and bills, documentation of lost income, evidence of disability, and any other records that paint a clear picture of your situation.

content.text: Special Tax Protections for Veterans and Military Personnel

Members of the military and veterans receive special treatment under federal tax law — and these provisions can significantly affect tax debt situations. Understanding what protections apply to you is critical.
The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act

The SCRA provides important financial protections for active duty military members, including a cap of 6% on interest rates on certain debts incurred before active duty and protections from certain civil legal proceedings.
Combat Zone Tax Exclusion
Military members serving in designated combat zones can exclude their combat pay from federal income tax — a significant benefit, especially for higher-ranking service members.
VA Disability Compensation and Taxes

VA disability compensation is not taxable and is specifically excluded from gross income. However, military retirement pay is generally taxable. Many veterans confuse these two and either over-report or under-report income.
IRS Relief for Veterans Facing Financial Hardship

Veterans experiencing financial hardship often qualify for Currently Not Collectible status due to disability, limited income, or high medical expenses. content.text: Separating Fact from Fiction

You have seen the ads: settle your $100,000 tax debt for just $500. They appear everywhere, promising miraculous IRS settlements for a fraction of what you owe. Is there any truth to this? Yes — but the fine print matters enormously.
When It Is Real

The Offer in Compromise program really does allow taxpayers to settle for far less than they owe — sometimes dramatically less. SympleTax has negotiated settlements where clients paid 1 to 5% of their original balance. But these outcomes result from specific qualifying circumstances, not marketing claims.
The Qualification Reality

The IRS accepts approximately 40% of OIC applications. To qualify for a very low settlement, your income must be very low relative to your debt, your assets must be minimal, and your future earning potential must be limited.
What Predatory Companies Actually Do

Some tax relief companies charge large upfront fees, submit an OIC they know will be rejected, drag the process out for months or years, and eventually tell you the IRS would not cooperate. Meanwhile, interest and penalties continue accruing on your balance.
The SympleTax Commitment

At SympleTax, we only recommend the Offer in Compromise when we genuinely believe you qualify — and our fee structure reflects realistic outcomes, not fantasy promises. content.text: You Have the Right to Appeal

Many taxpayers do not realize that IRS decisions are not final. If you disagree with an IRS examination, determination, or collection action, you have the right to appeal through the IRS Office of Appeals — an independent body that resolves disputes without litigation.
Types of Appealable Issues

You can appeal audit results, rejected Offers in Compromise, denied penalty abatement requests, collection actions through the Collection Due Process hearing, and trust fund recovery penalty determinations.
The Collection Due Process Hearing

The CDP hearing is one of the most powerful tools available. When you receive a Final Notice of Intent to Levy, you have 30 days to request a CDP hearing. This stops most IRS collection action and gives you the right to propose alternative collection solutions.
IRS Office of Appeals

Your case is assigned to an IRS Appeals Officer with no prior involvement in your case. Appeals are intended to be a fresh, impartial review. Settlement rates at the appeals level are high — many cases are resolved without going to Tax Court.
Tax Court as a Last Resort

If you cannot reach agreement through Appeals, you can petition the U.S. Tax Court. For amounts under $50,000, the Small Tax Case procedure is faster and designed for taxpayers without attorneys — though professional representation still helps significantly. Do not accept an IRS decision without exploring your appeal rights. SympleTax handles IRS appeals — contact us to review your options. You may have more rights than you realize. Schedule a free consultation with SympleTax and we will evaluate your appeal options.

content.text: Your Most Powerful Protection Against IRS Collection

The Collection Due Process (CDP) hearing is one of the strongest tools available to taxpayers facing IRS collection action. When exercised properly, it can stop the IRS in its tracks and open the door to resolution alternatives.
When Can You Request One?

You can request a CDP hearing after receiving a Notice of Federal Tax Lien Filing and Your Right to a Hearing, or a Final Notice of Intent to Levy. You have exactly 30 days from the date on these notices to submit your request.
What Happens During a CDP Hearing?

CDP hearings are conducted by the IRS Office of Appeals. You can raise collection alternatives including installment agreements, OIC, or CNC status. You can also challenge whether the underlying tax was properly assessed or argue that the collection action is inappropriate.
Effect on IRS Collection
Filing a timely CDP request immediately suspends most IRS levy action until the hearing is concluded. This gives you critical time to negotiate a resolution without the threat of immediate seizure.
Equivalent Hearings for Late Filers

If you miss the 30-day window, you can request an Equivalent Hearing within one year. However, the IRS is not required to suspend collection during an Equivalent Hearing — so timeliness is critical. If you have received a Final Notice of Intent to Levy, the clock is ticking. Contact SympleTax immediately — we file CDP requests and protect clients every day. Do not let the 30-day window close on you. Reach out to SympleTax right now and we will take action before your deadline passes.

content.text: The Penalty Relief Most Taxpayers Do Not Know About

The IRS has a penalty relief program called First-Time Abatement that is remarkably easy to qualify for — yet most taxpayers never take advantage of it. If you have received a failure-to-file, failure-to-pay, or failure-to-deposit penalty, you may be eligible to have it completely removed.
Who Qualifies?
To qualify, you must have no penalties in the prior three tax years (other than estimated tax penalties), have filed all required returns, and have paid or arranged to pay any tax currently due.
How to Request It

FTA can be requested by calling the IRS directly (the fastest method), by writing a letter, or through a tax professional. When calling, reference the First-Time Abatement policy specifically. Most IRS agents will process it immediately if you qualify.
Limitations of FTA

FTA applies to only one tax year per application and cannot be used for the same period where Reasonable Cause abatement was already granted. If you have penalties on multiple years, FTA can typically be applied to the oldest year first.
How Much Can FTA Save?

Failure-to-file and failure-to-pay penalties can reach 25% of unpaid tax each — meaning penalties can equal 50% of your original balance. On a $20,000 balance, FTA could eliminate $10,000 in penalties. You may have a free pass on penalties you did not know about. content.text: When the IRS Comes for Your Home

Your home is likely your most valuable asset — and the IRS knows it. Federal tax liens attach to all real property you own, which can complicate refinancing, selling, and obtaining a home equity loan.
Tax Liens and Your Mortgage

When you try to refinance or sell your home, the title company will discover any outstanding federal tax liens in the public record. A lien means you cannot transfer clear title to a buyer without first resolving the lien.
Discharge of Property

A discharge removes the federal tax lien from a specific piece of property so a sale can proceed. The IRS may agree if the proceeds from the sale will satisfy the lien, or if the government's interest has no value because your equity is less than the lien amount.
Using Home Sale Proceeds to Fund an OIC

Many clients use proceeds from selling a home to fund an Offer in Compromise settlement. The key is to negotiate the OIC before the sale closes so the IRS lien is discharged as part of the settlement.
Can the IRS Force a Home Sale?

The IRS can seize and sell a primary residence — but it is rare and requires court approval. The IRS prefers to work with taxpayers rather than pursue this extreme remedy. However, ignoring the IRS indefinitely can eventually lead there. Your home does not have to be at risk. content.text: Can You Inherit Someone's Tax Debt?

When a loved one passes, discovering they left behind significant tax debt adds pain to grief. Understanding how inherited tax debt works — and what your responsibilities actually are — can prevent costly mistakes.
Estate Liability vs. Personal Heir Liability

Generally, heirs are not personally liable for a deceased person's tax debt. The debt is owed by the estate, not the beneficiaries personally. However, if you receive assets from the estate before the estate's tax debts are paid, the IRS can recover those assets up to the value you received.
Federal Estate Tax

The federal estate tax applies only to very large estates — those exceeding $13.61 million per individual in 2024. Most Americans do not owe federal estate tax. However, state estate taxes have lower thresholds in many states.
Inherited IRAs and Tax Implications

When you inherit a traditional IRA from someone other than a spouse, you generally must withdraw all funds within 10 years. These withdrawals are taxable income, meaning inheriting a large IRA can create a significant tax liability of its own.
If You Are Administering an Estate With Tax Debt

If you are the executor of an estate with IRS debt, you have a legal obligation to apply estate assets to the tax debt before distributing to heirs. Distributing assets while ignoring the IRS debt can result in personal liability. Estate tax issues require expert guidance from the start. Contact SympleTax and we will help you navigate the estate's tax obligations correctly. Protecting beneficiaries while honoring obligations is our specialty. content.text: Myth 1: The IRS Will Accept Any Payment Plan You Propose

The IRS determines your minimum required monthly payment based on your financial situation. You can request an amount, but the IRS can and will reject proposals that do not reflect your actual disposable income.
Myth 2: Filing an Extension Gives You More Time to Pay

A tax filing extension gives you more time to file your return — not more time to pay. Taxes are still due on the original due date. If you do not pay by then, you will owe failure-to-pay penalties and interest from the original due date.
Myth 3: The IRS Cannot Touch Your Retirement Accounts

While retirement accounts have some protections in bankruptcy proceedings, the IRS can levy traditional IRAs and 401(k) accounts to satisfy tax debt. Protecting your retirement savings is a key priority at SympleTax.
Myth 4: If You Cannot Pay, Do Not File

This is one of the most costly misconceptions. The failure-to-file penalty is ten times larger than the failure-to-pay penalty. Always file your return, even if you cannot pay. You can work out the payment separately.
Myth 5: Tax Relief Is Only for Wealthy People

Tax relief programs were designed specifically for people with limited means — that is literally the eligibility criterion. Wealthy individuals with substantial assets rarely qualify for an Offer in Compromise. The truth about your tax situation may surprise you. Get the facts from SympleTax in a free consultation — no myths, just honest answers. Misconceptions cost taxpayers thousands every year. content.text: How Tax Debt Threatens Business Credit

For business owners, tax debt is not just a personal problem — it is a business problem. Federal tax liens can attach to business assets, appear in public records, and signal to lenders, vendors, and partners that your business may be in financial trouble.
Business Tax Liens in the Public Record

Federal tax liens are public documents filed with county recorders' offices. A search by any lender, supplier, or business partner can reveal your lien. This affects business credit lines, supplier terms, commercial leases, and business insurance.
Trust Fund Recovery Penalty and Business Credit
If the IRS assesses the Trust Fund Recovery Penalty against you personally for unpaid payroll taxes, this creates a personal tax lien that follows you even after the business closes.
Recovery Steps After Resolving Business Tax Debt

After resolving business tax debt, confirm lien release and ensure public records are updated, rebuild business credit through secured accounts and vendor relationships, establish a strong payroll tax compliance system, and work with an accountant to implement quarterly tax planning.

content.text: Raiding Retirement to Pay the IRS: Is It Worth It?

When facing IRS collection action, many taxpayers turn to their retirement accounts as a source of funds. While this can sometimes make sense, it often creates a new tax problem just as large as the original one.
The Double Tax Problem

When you take an early withdrawal from a traditional IRA or 401(k) before age 59½, you face regular income tax on the full withdrawal amount plus a 10% early withdrawal penalty. If you are in the 22% tax bracket and withdraw $20,000, you effectively pay $6,400 to access those funds — and then use them to pay a different IRS debt.
When It Might Make Sense

Using retirement funds can make sense if you are over 59½ (no early withdrawal penalty), the retirement account is your only significant asset, and paying down the tax debt avoids penalties, interest, and collection action that would cost more in the long run.
Hardship Exceptions to the 10% Penalty
The IRS recognizes several exceptions including disability, substantially equal periodic payments, certain medical expenses, and birth or adoption expenses.
Better Alternatives in Most Cases

In most cases, IRS resolution programs — particularly Currently Not Collectible status or an Offer in Compromise — cost far less than depleting retirement accounts. Before touching your retirement savings, explore every IRS resolution option. Talk to SympleTax first — a free consultation could save you tens of thousands. content.text: The Client's Situation

Maria R. was a 34-year-old single mother of two working as a medical assistant in Houston, Texas. After a difficult divorce three years earlier, Maria had been filing taxes inconsistently — some years she filed and could not pay, other years she did not file at all, buried under the weight of raising her children alone on a single income.
Finding SympleTax

When Maria finally opened all the IRS notices she had been pushing to the back of a drawer, the total balance — including penalties and interest — had grown to $127,400. She was terrified. She had no savings, no home equity, and barely enough to cover rent, childcare, and groceries. She thought the IRS would garnish her wages and she would lose her apartment.
The Investigation

Our licensed team pulled Maria's complete IRS account transcripts and discovered that two years had been filed as Substitute for Returns by the IRS — without Maria's deductions or exemptions. By properly filing those returns, we reduced her actual tax liability by over $18,000 before we even began the resolution process. Additionally, over $42,000 of her balance was penalties and interest, much of which was abatable.
The Strategy: Two-Phase Approach

Phase 1: We filed corrected returns for the years with Substitute for Returns and simultaneously filed for Penalty Abatement under Reasonable Cause for the years Maria had been unable to file due to her divorce crisis. Phase 2: We submitted an Offer in Compromise based on Maria's true financial profile — $3,200 per month in income, $3,050 in allowable monthly expenses for housing, childcare, food, transportation, and healthcare, leaving just $150 in monthly disposable income. Her assets were minimal: a vehicle with $2,000 in equity, no savings, no real estate.
The Outcome

The IRS accepted Maria's Offer in Compromise for $850 — paid in a lump sum using a modest tax refund she received after her corrected returns were processed. Her $127,400 balance was legally resolved for less than 1% of what she originally owed. Maria called our office in tears when she received the acceptance letter. Today she is fully compliant with her taxes, has automatic withholding from her paycheck, and is building her savings for the first time in years.
Key Takeaways From Maria's Case

Substitute for Returns almost always overstate what you owe — filing correctly is critical A two-phase approach of correction plus resolution often achieves far better outcomes than jumping straight to an OIC Childcare expenses are allowable and significantly reduce the IRS's view of disposable income Acting on the Final Notice of Intent to Levy rather than waiting preserved Maria's options If Maria's story resonates with you, do not wait. content.text: The Client's Situation

Dorothy W. was 82 years old, a widow living alone in Phoenix, Arizona on $1,650 per month in Social Security benefits. Her late husband Harold had managed all their finances during their 54-year marriage. After Harold passed away, Dorothy discovered he had not filed tax returns for the last four years of his life. With no knowledge of taxes and limited computer skills, she had been unable to navigate the mounting IRS notices. The total balance had grown to $94,200 — and the IRS intended to levy 15% of her Social Security.
The Discovery: A Massive IRS Error

When SympleTax pulled Dorothy's IRS transcripts, we identified the core issue immediately: the IRS had improperly included VA disability compensation Harold received in the Substitute for Returns. VA disability compensation is explicitly excluded from gross income by federal law. By filing correct returns for all four years, Dorothy's actual tax liability dropped from $94,200 to approximately $41,000.
Penalty Abatement

We filed a detailed Penalty Abatement request citing Harold's declining health and Dorothy's complete lack of tax knowledge as Reasonable Cause. The IRS agreed to abate $22,800 in penalties and interest, bringing the balance to $18,200.
The OIC Analysis

Dorothy's monthly income was $1,650 in Social Security. Her allowable expenses — housing, food, medical, transportation — totaled $1,580. With only $70 per month in disposable income and minimal assets, we submitted an Offer in Compromise for $1,200.
The Outcome

The IRS accepted Dorothy's Offer in Compromise for $1,200. Her $94,200 IRS debt was legally extinguished for 1.3% of the original balance. The Social Security levy was permanently released and the lien was cleared within 30 days of payment. Dorothy's daughter sent us a handwritten thank-you note: her mother was sleeping again, eating again, and back to herself.
Key Takeaways

The death of a spouse creates immediate tax filing obligations that surviving spouses need professional guidance to navigate IRS Substitute for Returns frequently contain errors that significantly overstate the balance Penalty Abatement under Reasonable Cause can dramatically reduce balances before pursuing an OIC
Seniors on fixed incomes are often ideal Offer in Compromise candidates

content.text: The Client's Situation

James T. had built his Atlanta restaurant from nothing. At its peak it employed 23 people. Then the pandemic hit. Forced to close and then reopen at limited capacity, James made a desperate decision: he kept his employees on payroll using funds that should have been remitted to the IRS as payroll taxes, telling himself he would pay it back when business recovered. Business never fully recovered. By the time he closed the restaurant, he had accumulated $212,000 in unpaid payroll taxes across six quarters.
The Challenge: Personal Liability

The Trust Fund Recovery Penalty assessment held James personally liable for the trust fund portion of the payroll taxes — approximately $138,000 — even after the business closed. This debt follows the individual, survives bankruptcy, and cannot be escaped by simply closing the company.
SympleTax's Multi-Pronged Strategy

First, we reviewed James's personal liability assessment and identified that two of his former managers had also been assessed with TFRP, meaning James was not solely responsible for all six quarters. We successfully appealed a portion of his TFRP assessment, reducing his personal liability. Second, we negotiated directly with the IRS on the business entity's remaining balance, establishing that the closed business had no assets to collect from. Third, we submitted a personal Offer in Compromise for James based on his post-restaurant financial situation.
The Outcome

The IRS accepted James's Offer in Compromise for $8,500, settling his personal liability on a $212,000 business tax debt. He also received penalty abatement on the employer-side taxes, and the federal tax lien was withdrawn after OIC completion. James has since launched a restaurant consulting business and credits SympleTax with giving him the financial foundation to start over.
Key Takeaways
The Trust Fund Recovery Penalty can be appealed — not every assessment is accurate
Closed businesses still create IRS liability issues that must be properly addressed
A personal OIC can resolve TFRP liability even after business closure
Pandemic-era financial hardship remains a legitimate basis for IRS relief

content.text: The Client's Situation

Robert V. served 12 years in the U.S. Army, including two combat tours. After leaving the service, Robert struggled with PTSD and a service-connected physical disability. He received VA disability compensation and a small military retirement stipend. He had not filed federal tax returns for seven years — not from willful avoidance, but because the anxiety associated with his PTSD made opening the IRS notices impossible. When a friend finally helped him sort through the stack, the total balance showed $85,000.
A Massive IRS Error Discovered

When SympleTax pulled Robert's IRS transcripts, we immediately identified the core problem: the IRS had improperly included VA disability compensation in Robert's income on the Substitute for Return assessments. VA disability compensation is explicitly excluded from gross income under federal law. By filing correct returns for all seven years, Robert's actual taxable income for several of those years was zero or near-zero. His actual tax liability dropped from $85,000 to approximately $9,200.
Penalty Abatement for Reasonable Cause

We filed a detailed Penalty Abatement request citing Robert's diagnosed PTSD as the Reasonable Cause for failure to file and respond to IRS notices. We included VA documentation, disability rating, treatment records, and a letter from his mental health provider. The IRS granted abatement of $6,800 in penalties and interest, reducing the balance to $2,400.
Currently Not Collectible Status

Even $2,400 was genuinely unaffordable. We filed for Currently Not Collectible hardship status, documenting that Robert's monthly income was entirely consumed by allowable living expenses. The IRS granted CNC status — suspending all collection activity indefinitely. With the CSED clock continuing to run, Robert's remaining balance will legally expire without a single additional dollar paid.
Key Takeaways
VA disability compensation is not taxable — IRS errors on this are common and correctable
Mental health conditions like PTSD qualify as Reasonable Cause for penalty abatement
CNC status combined with the CSED expiration can mean paying nothing at all

content.text: The Client's Situation

Alicia M. was a 28-year-old aspiring entrepreneur in Miami who had launched a social media marketing agency at 22 with impressive early success. But she had no accounting background and no understanding of quarterly estimated taxes or self-employment tax. For three consecutive years she earned substantial income, paid nothing to the IRS, and did not file. By the time a Final Notice of Intent to Levy arrived, the total balance had reached $103,000.
Phase 1: Get Compliant

We properly filed all three years of returns with full Schedule C deductions. The IRS's SFR balance of $103,000 dropped to approximately $58,000 of actual tax liability after accounting for missed deductions.
Phase 2: Penalty Abatement

For subsequent years, we documented Reasonable Cause based on economic disruption during the pandemic. The IRS agreed, abating an additional $14,700 in penalties. title: What Is the IRS Taxpayer Advocate Service and How Can It Help You?

content.text: An Independent Voice Inside the IRS

Most taxpayers don't know there's an independent organization inside the IRS specifically designed to protect their rights. The Taxpayer Advocate Service (TAS) is a free, confidential resource that helps taxpayers resolve problems they haven't been able to fix on their own — and it can be a powerful ally when the IRS system seems to be working against you.
What TAS Does

TAS helps taxpayers who are experiencing financial difficulty due to IRS actions, who have tried to resolve their issue through normal IRS channels without success, or who believe the IRS has made an error. Each state has at least one Local Taxpayer Advocate office, and there is a national office in Washington, D.C.
When to Contact TAS

TAS is most effective when you are facing immediate financial harm — an active levy that's preventing you from paying rent or buying food, an IRS error that has persisted for months, a processing delay that is affecting your financial situation, or when the IRS has not responded to your attempts to resolve an issue within the timeframes they've committed to.

items: Imminent threat of adverse action (levy, lien, seizure)
You've already tried the normal IRS process without resolution
You're experiencing significant economic hardship
The IRS system has failed you procedurally

content.text: How to Request TAS Help

You can contact TAS by calling 1-877-777-4778, visiting your local TAS office, or submitting Form 911 (Request for Taxpayer Advocate Service Assistance). TAS is free and your information is kept confidential. They will assign you a case advocate who will work on your case and keep you informed.
TAS vs. Hiring a Professional

TAS is a valuable resource, but it has limitations. They cannot advocate for legal positions that don't have merit, and they work within IRS rules — they cannot negotiate a settlement the way a licensed tax professional can. For complex tax debt situations, using TAS in conjunction with a tax relief firm like SympleTax often produces the best results. We know exactly when to involve TAS and how to coordinate efforts. title: What Happens to Your Tax Debt When You Move to Another Country?
excerpt: Many Americans believe that moving overseas will put their tax problems behind them. The reality is quite different. The United States is one of only two countries in the world (along with Eritrea) that taxes its citizens on worldwide income regardless of where they live. If…

content.text: The IRS Follows You Everywhere

Many Americans believe that moving overseas will put their tax problems behind them. The reality is quite different. The United States is one of only two countries in the world (along with Eritrea) that taxes its citizens on worldwide income regardless of where they live. If you're a U.S. citizen or green card holder, you have federal tax filing and payment obligations no matter what country you call home.
FBAR and FATCA: Additional Overseas Reporting Requirements

Living abroad adds new reporting requirements on top of your regular tax obligations. FBAR (FinCEN Form 114) requires disclosure of foreign bank accounts if the aggregate value exceeds $10,000 at any point during the year. FATCA (Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act, Form 8938) requires disclosure of foreign financial assets above certain thresholds. Failure to file these can result in penalties of $10,000 or more per violation.
Can the IRS Collect Abroad?

Yes. The IRS has tax treaties with numerous countries that include provisions for collection assistance. Additionally, if you try to renew your passport with significant tax debt (over $59,000 threshold), the State Department can deny or revoke it — effectively trapping you at home or stranding you abroad. Tax liens also apply to foreign assets in many circumstances.
Options for Americans Living Abroad With IRS Debt

Expatriates with IRS debt have access to the same resolution programs as domestic taxpayers — Offer in Compromise, Installment Agreements, and Currently Not Collectible status. Additionally, the IRS Streamlined Foreign Offshore Procedures offer reduced penalties for non-willful compliance failures by those living abroad. Acting before the IRS escalates is critical.
SympleTax Helps Expats

SympleTax works with American citizens abroad to resolve IRS debt and bring them into compliance. Whether you're in Canada, Europe, Asia, or anywhere else — your U.S. tax obligations don't stop at the border, and neither does our ability to help you. content.text: Love and Tax Liability

Getting married is one of the most exciting decisions you'll ever make. But if your future spouse carries IRS debt — or if you do — understanding how marriage affects tax liability is essential. Some surprises here can be costly, but most can be managed with the right knowledge.
Injured Spouse vs. Innocent Spouse: What's the Difference?

These two terms sound similar but address very different situations. Innocent Spouse Relief protects you from liability on a joint return where your spouse caused the debt through error or fraud. Injured Spouse Relief (Form 8379) is different — it protects your portion of a joint refund from being seized to pay your spouse's pre-marital tax debt. If your spouse had IRS debt before you married, you can file as an injured spouse to protect your share of any joint refund.
Filing Status Choices After Marriage

Once married, you can file jointly or separately. Filing jointly typically results in lower taxes but creates joint liability for any balance due. Filing separately protects you from your spouse's tax issues but comes at a cost: you lose access to many valuable credits and deductions, and tax rates for Married Filing Separately are higher than for joint filers.
What If Your Spouse Already Has an Installment Agreement?

If your spouse is already in an IRS installment agreement or other resolution program, that generally does not affect your separate tax obligations. However, if you file jointly, your refunds could be applied to their debt — making injured spouse protection critically important.
Pre-Nuptial Tax Planning

Before combining finances, consider having a conversation — and a professional consultation — about any existing tax issues. Knowing the full picture before marriage allows you to make informed decisions about filing status, protect your own finances, and develop a plan to resolve any existing debt together or separately. content.text: The IRS Has Gone Digital

The IRS has significantly expanded its online tools in recent years, making it easier than ever to view your balance, make payments, set up installment agreements, and track notices — all from your computer or phone. Understanding what these tools can and can't do helps you use them effectively.
IRS Online Account

The IRS Online Account (available at IRS.gov/account) lets you view your tax records and account balance, see payment history and pending payments, access prior year tax transcripts, view digital versions of key IRS notices, and set up or modify a payment plan. Setting up an account requires identity verification through ID.me.
IRS Direct Pay

IRS Direct Pay allows you to pay your taxes directly from a bank account at no charge. It's available 24/7 and provides instant confirmation. You can pay balance dues, estimated taxes, or installment agreement payments. Direct Pay does not require you to create an IRS account.
Setting Up a Payment Plan Online

If you owe $50,000 or less (for individuals), you can apply for an online installment agreement through the IRS website. The process takes about 15 minutes and provides immediate approval in most cases. However, the IRS's online system won't tell you whether you'd be better served by an Offer in Compromise or another program — it simply offers you a payment plan.
When DIY Isn't Enough

Online IRS tools are useful for simple situations — checking a balance, making a payment, setting up a basic payment plan. But if you're facing significant debt, unfiled returns, penalties, levy threats, or complex financial circumstances, the online tools don't evaluate your full range of options. SympleTax does. We ensure you're not agreeing to a payment plan when you might qualify to settle for far less. title: How the IRS Calculates Interest on Your Tax Debt (And How to Stop It)
excerpt: The moment you have an unpaid tax balance, interest begins accruing. Unlike penalties, which often have caps, interest compounds daily and never stops until your balance is paid in full. Understanding how IRS interest works — and how to stop it — can save you thousands of…
author: SympleTax Team

metaDescription: The moment you have an unpaid tax balance, interest begins accruing. Unlike penalties, which often have caps, interest compounds daily and never stops until y…

content.text: The Interest Clock Starts Immediately

The moment you have an unpaid tax balance, interest begins accruing. Unlike penalties, which often have caps, interest compounds daily and never stops until your balance is paid in full. Understanding how IRS interest works — and how to stop it — can save you thousands of dollars.
The IRS Interest Rate Formula

The IRS charges interest at the federal short-term rate (set quarterly) plus 3 percentage points. In recent years, as federal interest rates rose, the IRS interest rate climbed to 8% or higher. On a $50,000 balance, that's $4,000 per year in interest alone — before penalties are considered.
Interest Is Not Abatable (Usually)

Unlike penalties, interest generally cannot be abated — it's a statutory charge that the IRS has no discretion to waive, with a narrow exception called 'interest abatement' that applies only when the interest results from IRS errors or delays. This is rare. The most effective way to stop interest is to resolve the underlying balance.
How an OIC Stops Interest

When you submit an Offer in Compromise, the IRS suspends collection activity — including the accrual of additional collection costs — while your case is evaluated. If your OIC is accepted, the IRS writes off all remaining interest (and penalties) above your settlement amount. This is one of the most powerful financial benefits of a successful OIC.
Partial Payments and Interest Reduction

Even if you can't resolve your full balance immediately, making partial payments reduces the principal and therefore reduces the interest base. In an installment agreement, your minimum required payment should always exceed the monthly interest accrual — otherwise your balance will grow even as you make payments. SympleTax calculates optimal payment amounts to ensure clients are actually reducing their balance, not treading water.
