content.text: Business Non-Filing: A More Serious Problem

When an individual doesn't file personal tax returns, the consequences are serious. When a business fails to file required returns — whether corporate income tax, partnership returns, S-corporation returns, or employment tax returns — the consequences can be even more severe and complex.
Types of Required Business Returns

Depending on your business structure, you may be required to file: Form 1120 (C-Corporation), Form 1120-S (S-Corporation), Form 1065 (Partnership/Multi-Member LLC), Form 940 (Annual Federal Unemployment Tax), Form 941 (Quarterly Payroll Tax), and various state equivalents. Missing any of these creates separate penalties and IRS issues.
Failure to File Penalties for Businesses

Business failure-to-file penalties can be severe. For partnership returns, the penalty is $235 per month per partner (2024 rate) — a 5-partner LLC can accumulate $1,175 per month in penalties for a single missed return. For S-corporations, the same per-shareholder penalty applies.
The IRS's Substitute for Return for Businesses

The IRS can file a Substitute for Return for businesses just as they do for individuals. For C-Corporations, this assessment ignores deductible business expenses, depreciation, and officer salaries — creating a dramatically overstated tax liability. Properly filing the actual return almost always results in a dramatically lower balance.
Getting Current: The Voluntary Path

The best approach for a non-filing business is to get compliant proactively before the IRS takes aggressive action. SympleTax works with business owners to reconstruct records, prepare all missing returns, negotiate penalty abatement, and develop a resolution plan for any balance due. Getting current voluntarily typically results in far better outcomes than waiting for IRS enforcement. title: How to Read Your IRS Tax Transcript: A Complete Guide

content.text: Your IRS Transcript Is Your Tax History

Your IRS tax transcript is the most authoritative record of your tax account — it shows every assessment, payment, penalty, adjustment, and action taken on your account. Tax professionals rely heavily on transcripts to understand a client's situation, identify errors, and develop resolution strategies. Here's how to read one.
Types of IRS Transcripts
The IRS offers several types of transcripts:

items: Tax Return Transcript — shows most line items from your filed return
Tax Account Transcript — shows all account activity, assessments, payments, adjustments, and penalties
Record of Account Transcript — combines the return and account transcripts
Wage and Income Transcript — shows all income reported to the IRS under your Social Security Number
Verification of Non-Filing Letter — confirms no return was filed for a specific year

content.text: Key Transaction Codes to Know

IRS transcripts use three-digit transaction codes. Key codes include: 150 (return filed and tax assessed), 290 (additional tax assessed), 300-309 (tax examination changes), 420 (examination in progress), 530 (currently not collectible status applied), 780/781 (OIC submitted/rejected), 971/972 (notice issued/rescinded). Understanding these codes reveals the full story of your account.
How to Get Your Transcripts

Transcripts are available through the IRS Online Account portal, by calling the IRS at 1-800-908-9946, by submitting Form 4506-T, or through your authorized tax professional using IRS e-Services. SympleTax obtains full transcripts for every client as a first step — it's impossible to develop a sound resolution strategy without them.
What Transcripts Reveal That You Might Not Know

Transcripts often reveal surprises: assessments you didn't know about, payments that weren't properly credited, statute of limitations dates that are closer than you thought, or IRS errors that have inflated your balance. A trained eye can identify issues and opportunities that the average taxpayer would completely miss. content.text: The Form That Changes Everything

When you apply for an Offer in Compromise, request a non-streamlined installment agreement, or seek Currently Not Collectible status, the IRS requires a detailed financial statement. Form 433 — and its various versions — is the document that reveals your complete financial picture to the IRS and determines what resolution options you qualify for. The Different Versions of Form 433
There are several versions of Form 433:

items: Form 433-A — for individuals and self-employed persons
Form 433-B — for businesses
Form 433-F — a simplified version used for basic installment agreements
Form 433-A (OIC) — specifically for Offer in Compromise applications

content.text: What Form 433 Captures

Form 433-A collects information about your income from all sources, monthly living expenses, bank account balances, real estate and equity, vehicles and their values, investments and retirement accounts, business interests, and any other assets. The completeness and accuracy of this form is critical — omissions can result in rejection or criminal fraud allegations.
The Art of Completing Form 433 Strategically

How you complete Form 433 matters enormously. Expenses must be properly categorized and documented. Allowable expense standards must be applied correctly. Asset values should reflect fair market value minus selling costs and encumbrances. Many taxpayers — and some inexperienced preparers — leave money on the table by not fully documenting allowable expenses or by overstating asset values.
Why Professional Help Is Critical

SympleTax's licensed professionals have completed thousands of IRS financial statements. We know which expenses are allowable, how to document them compellingly, and how to present your financial picture in the most accurate and favorable light possible. A poorly completed Form 433 can result in a rejected OIC or an installment agreement with unaffordable payments. A well-completed one is the foundation of a successful resolution. content.text: Essential Workers and Tax Challenges

Nurses, teachers, police officers, firefighters, and paramedics dedicate their lives to serving others — often at significant personal and financial sacrifice. These professions come with unique tax situations, and many essential workers find themselves in tax debt through no fault of their own: overtime fluctuations, union dues, job transitions, and side income all create complexity that standard tax preparation often misses.
Common Tax Issues for Healthcare Workers

Travel nurses face particularly complex tax situations — multi-state income, per diem allowances, tax home maintenance requirements, and the shifting landscape of travel nursing tax rules post-pandemic have left many nurses with unexpected back-tax bills.

items: Multi-state income requires filing multiple state returns
Travel nurse tax-free stipends have strict requirements that, if violated, create taxable income
Agency-to-agency transitions can create W-2 complexity
Overtime and bonus income often isn't adequately withheld

content.text: Teachers: Above-the-Line Deductions Often Missed

Teachers can deduct up to $300 ($600 if married filing jointly, both teachers) in classroom expenses as an above-the-line deduction — meaning you don't have to itemize. Additional classroom expenses beyond this cap may qualify as miscellaneous deductions in some circumstances. Many teachers also incur professional development costs that are potentially deductible.
First Responders and Disability Income

First responders who receive disability benefits face complex tax treatment depending on whether benefits come from employer plans, public safety officer provisions, or workers' compensation. Public Safety Officer disability benefits are often excluded from taxable income — a provision many first responders don't know about.
When Essential Workers Owe the IRS

When a nurse, teacher, or first responder accumulates tax debt, the same relief programs apply — but SympleTax understands the specific income patterns and allowable deductions relevant to these professions. We've helped essential workers resolve significant tax debts while maximizing every profession-specific deduction available. content.text: When Disaster Strikes, the IRS Steps Back

In the aftermath of federally declared natural disasters, the IRS provides significant tax relief to affected taxpayers. Filing deadlines are extended, penalties are suspended, and special deductions become available. Understanding what relief you're entitled to after a disaster can make a meaningful difference in your recovery.
Automatic Filing and Payment Extensions

When a federal disaster declaration is issued, the IRS typically automatically extends filing and payment deadlines for taxpayers in the affected area — no formal request is required. Extensions are often 90 days to a full year. Affected taxpayers are identified by their registered address in IRS records.
The Casualty Loss Deduction

Disaster victims who suffered property losses may be able to claim a casualty loss deduction on their federal return. After the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, this deduction is currently limited to losses in federally declared disaster areas and must exceed 10% of your Adjusted Gross Income (after a $100 reduction). Despite these limitations, the deduction can be substantial for serious losses.
Early Retirement Withdrawals After Disasters

Special legislation following major disasters often allows taxpayers to withdraw up to $22,000 from retirement accounts without the 10% early withdrawal penalty, and to spread the income inclusion over three years. This provision has applied after several recent disaster declarations.
Pre-Existing Tax Debt and Disasters

If you already had IRS tax debt when a disaster struck, the disaster may provide grounds for penalty abatement under Reasonable Cause, modification of installment agreements based on changed financial circumstances, and temporary suspension of collection activity. SympleTax has helped disaster survivors use these provisions to restructure existing tax debt while navigating the broader recovery process. excerpt: Rental income — from long-term tenants, Airbnb, VRBO, or any other arrangement — is taxable federal income that must be reported on Schedule E of your tax return. Many landlords, particularly those with small rental operations, fail to report this income or misunderstand what is…

content.text: Rental Income Is Always Taxable

Rental income — from long-term tenants, Airbnb, VRBO, or any other arrangement — is taxable federal income that must be reported on Schedule E of your tax return. Many landlords, particularly those with small rental operations, fail to report this income or misunderstand what is and isn't deductible, creating tax bills they didn't expect.
The Most Common Landlord Tax Mistakes

Failing to report cash rental income, misclassifying repairs as capital improvements (or vice versa), not claiming depreciation, incorrectly allocating expenses between personal and rental use (for mixed-use properties), and failing to report rental income from short-term rental platforms that issue 1099-Ks — these are the most common issues that bring landlords to the IRS's attention.
Depreciation: Your Most Powerful Landlord Deduction

The IRS allows landlords to depreciate residential rental property over 27.5 years. On a $275,000 rental property, that's $10,000 per year in depreciation — a paper deduction that can offset significant rental income. Many landlords miss this deduction, and when they sell, they face 'depreciation recapture' on gains — a tax that applies even if they never claimed the deduction.
Passive Activity Loss Rules and Tax Debt

Rental losses are generally considered 'passive' and can only offset other passive income — unless your income is under $100,000 and you actively participate in managing the property (in which case you can deduct up to $25,000 of rental losses against ordinary income). Real estate professionals who spend more than 750 hours per year in real estate activities can deduct all rental losses against any income.
When Rental Property Creates IRS Debt

Landlords with IRS debt face a unique challenge: rental properties are visible, valuable assets that the IRS considers in any financial analysis. SympleTax helps landlords structure their IRS resolution in ways that protect their rental portfolio, correctly account for mortgage debt and property values, and present the most accurate and favorable financial picture possible. title: IRS Wage Garnishment vs. Bank Levy: Understanding the Difference
excerpt: When the IRS decides to take enforced collection action, they have two primary tools for reaching money you have now: wage garnishments and bank levies. While both are devastating, they work differently — and stopping them requires different strategies. Understanding which…
author: SympleTax Team

metaDescription: When the IRS decides to take enforced collection action, they have two primary tools for reaching money you have now: wage garnishments and bank levies. While…

content.text: Two Ways the IRS Takes Your Money

When the IRS decides to take enforced collection action, they have two primary tools for reaching money you have now: wage garnishments and bank levies. While both are devastating, they work differently — and stopping them requires different strategies. Understanding which you're facing (or at risk of) helps you respond correctly.
How a Wage Garnishment Works

A wage garnishment (technically a 'continuous levy') is served on your employer. Your employer then calculates your 'exempt amount' — a small portion of your pay that protects basic living — and sends everything above that directly to the IRS every pay period. It continues automatically until the IRS releases it. Many people see 50-70% of their net pay disappear.
How a Bank Levy Works

A bank levy is a one-time seizure. The IRS sends a levy notice to your bank, which immediately freezes the funds in your account up to the amount you owe. The bank holds those funds for 21 days before sending them to the IRS. During those 21 days, you have a window to act — but after the funds are sent, they're gone.
Which Is Harder to Reverse?

Bank levies are theoretically reversible within the 21-day window, but time is extremely short. Once funds are transferred to the IRS, getting them returned requires proving the levy was wrongful — which is rare. Wage garnishments are technically easier to reverse because they're ongoing — entering an installment agreement or other resolution immediately releases the garnishment. Speed matters equally in both cases.
Preventing Both: The Best Strategy

The best strategy is to prevent either from happening. The IRS sends a Final Notice of Intent to Levy before taking either action — and responding to that notice within 30 days is the surest way to prevent garnishment or levy. SympleTax monitors client accounts and responds aggressively to any escalating IRS action before it reaches the levy stage. title: How to Negotiate With the IRS: What Actually Works
excerpt: Many taxpayers assume the IRS is a rigid, non-negotiable bureaucracy. The reality is more nuanced. The IRS has established programs and procedures specifically designed to resolve debts that can't be paid in full — and within those frameworks, there is real room for negotiation.…

content.text: The IRS Is Negotiable — If You Know the Rules

Many taxpayers assume the IRS is a rigid, non-negotiable bureaucracy. The reality is more nuanced. The IRS has established programs and procedures specifically designed to resolve debts that can't be paid in full — and within those frameworks, there is real room for negotiation. Knowing the rules of engagement is the key.
What You Can and Cannot Negotiate

You can negotiate the amount of your settlement (OIC), monthly payment amounts in an installment agreement, penalty abatement (partially or fully), which tax periods to address first, and collection timeline and urgency. You generally cannot negotiate the underlying tax itself (though you can dispute it), interest on tax debts (with very limited exceptions), or trust fund taxes away from personal liability.
The IRS Responds to Financial Reality

The IRS is not emotional — they're practical. They want to collect as much as they realistically can. If you can demonstrate compellingly — with documentation — that your financial situation makes full collection impossible or impractical, the IRS will negotiate. The key word is 'demonstrate.' Claims without documentation are ignored; documented financial reality gets results.
Common Negotiation Mistakes

Offering more than you can afford (setting yourself up for default), volunteering information that hurts your case, agreeing to terms during a phone call before understanding your full options, and negotiating directly without professional representation are the most common mistakes that cost taxpayers thousands.

items: Never agree to anything in the first IRS call
Always get any IRS agreement in writing
Never volunteer assets or income the IRS didn't ask about
Always explore all options before accepting a payment plan

content.text: Two Debts the Government Can Both Collect

If you have both federal student loan debt and IRS tax debt, you're carrying two of the most powerful debts in existence — both owed to entities with extraordinary collection powers. The good news: both have resolution programs, and managing them strategically can make the load bearable.
How Student Loans and Tax Debt Interact

The Treasury Offset Program (TOP) allows the federal government to offset your tax refund to pay past-due student loans. If you expect a refund, it may be seized automatically. Additionally, if your student loans are in default, that can affect your financial picture in IRS resolution calculations — student loan payments are generally considered allowable expenses in IRS financial analysis.
Income-Driven Repayment and Taxes

Income-driven repayment plans (IDR) for student loans — such as SAVE, IBR, PAYE, and ICR — base your monthly payment on your income and family size. Importantly, the payments you make on IDR plans are an allowable expense in IRS financial analysis, which can reduce your calculated disposable income and improve your OIC eligibility or lower your required installment agreement payment.
Student Loan Forgiveness and Tax Implications

Public Service Loan Forgiveness (PSLF) results in tax-free forgiveness. However, income-driven repayment forgiveness (after 20-25 years) is generally taxable income at the federal level — meaning the year your loans are forgiven, you could face a massive tax bill. Planning ahead for this tax liability is critical. SympleTax helps clients anticipate and prepare for this.
Building a Coordinated Repayment Strategy

Managing IRS debt and student loan debt simultaneously requires a coordinated strategy that considers both the IRS's allowable expense standards and your student loan obligations. SympleTax works with clients to develop an integrated approach that resolves IRS debt while managing student loan obligations in the most financially efficient way possible. title: The IRS and Real Estate Sales: Capital Gains, Reporting, and What to Do When You Owe
excerpt: Real estate sales are one of the largest financial transactions most people ever make — and they can create significant tax obligations. Whether you're selling a primary home, investment property, or vacation home, understanding the tax implications before you close is far…

metaDescription: Real estate sales are one of the largest financial transactions most people ever make — and they can create significant tax obligations. Whether you're sellin…

content.text: Selling Real Estate Creates Tax Obligations

Real estate sales are one of the largest financial transactions most people ever make — and they can create significant tax obligations. Whether you're selling a primary home, investment property, or vacation home, understanding the tax implications before you close is far better than getting a surprise bill from the IRS afterward.
The Primary Home Exclusion

The most valuable provision in real estate taxation is the Section 121 exclusion: you can exclude up to $250,000 in capital gains ($500,000 for married couples filing jointly) on the sale of your primary residence, provided you've owned and used the home as your primary residence for at least 2 of the last 5 years. This exclusion eliminates taxes for many sellers entirely.
Investment Property and Capital Gains

For investment properties and second homes, the primary home exclusion doesn't apply. Long-term capital gains (property held over one year) are taxed at 0%, 15%, or 20% depending on your income level. Short-term gains (held one year or less) are taxed as ordinary income — potentially as high as 37%.
Depreciation Recapture: The Hidden Tax

If you've depreciated a rental property over the years (as the IRS requires), when you sell, the IRS 'recaptures' that depreciation at a maximum 25% tax rate — even if you never actually claimed the deductions. This depreciation recapture surprises many landlords and can add tens of thousands to the tax bill on a sale.
When Real Estate Sales Create IRS Debt

If you sell property and owe taxes you can't pay, all the same resolution programs apply. However, timing matters — the IRS may view your recent sale proceeds as an asset in OIC analysis. Coordinating a real estate sale with your IRS resolution strategy is something SympleTax does regularly. We help clients maximize after-tax proceeds while managing any resulting tax liability. title: How to Protect Your Assets from the IRS While Resolving Your Tax Debt
excerpt: The IRS's power to seize assets is real and broad. But it's also subject to specific rules and limitations — and there are legal, proactive strategies to protect your assets while working toward resolution. Understanding these protections is not about hiding assets (which is…

content.text: The IRS Has Broad Power — But It's Not Unlimited

The IRS's power to seize assets is real and broad. But it's also subject to specific rules and limitations — and there are legal, proactive strategies to protect your assets while working toward resolution. Understanding these protections is not about hiding assets (which is illegal) — it's about understanding what the law already shields.
Exempt Assets Under IRS Collection Rules
Certain assets have statutory protections from IRS levy:

items: Unemployment benefits
Workers' compensation payments
Certain pension and retirement income (partial protection)
A portion of wages (your exempt amount)
Primary residence (requires court approval to seize)
Tools of trade up to $3,520 in value
Books and educational materials up to $3,520

content.text: Retirement Accounts: More Protected Than You Think

While the IRS can technically levy retirement accounts, they rarely do so if a taxpayer is engaged in resolution. The IRS's own internal guidelines generally discourage seizure of retirement assets when other options exist. Furthermore, amounts needed to maintain a minimum standard of living can be argued as protected from levy.
The Importance of Not Transferring Assets to Avoid Collection

Transferring assets to family members or others to avoid IRS collection is illegal — the IRS can follow fraudulent transfers and reverse them. Furthermore, it can result in criminal fraud charges. Legal asset protection happens before tax debt arises, not after the IRS has already assessed it.
Resolution As the Best Protection

The most effective way to protect your assets from the IRS is to resolve your tax debt. An active installment agreement, pending OIC, or CNC status all generally prevent the IRS from pursuing asset seizure. content.text: Resolving Debt Is Step One — Staying Compliant Is Step Two

At SympleTax, we don't just resolve your current tax debt — we help you build the financial habits and systems to ensure you never face this situation again. Year-round tax planning isn't just for wealthy people. It's for anyone who wants to stay ahead of their obligations and out of the IRS's crosshairs.
The Four Quarterly Payment Deadlines You Must Know

If you're self-employed, have investment income, receive rental income, or have income from any source without withholding, you're required to make quarterly estimated tax payments. The deadlines are April 15, June 15, September 15, and January 15 of the following year. Missing these creates underpayment penalties on top of your regular tax bill.

items: Q1: January 1 – March 31 | Payment Due: April 15
Q2: April 1 – May 31 | Payment Due: June 15
Q3: June 1 – August 31 | Payment Due: September 15
Q4: September 1 – December 31 | Payment Due: January 15

content.text: The W-4 Strategy for Employees

If you're a W-2 employee, the biggest lever you have is your Form W-4 — the withholding form you give your employer. Too few allowances (or the wrong settings in the new format) means under-withholding and a tax bill at year-end. SympleTax can review your W-4 settings to ensure you're withholding the right amount.
Setting Aside the Right Percentage

A general rule of thumb: set aside 25-30% of all self-employment and freelance income for taxes. This covers federal income tax at moderate income levels and the full self-employment tax of 15.3%. Opening a separate savings account dedicated to taxes — and treating it as untouchable — is one of the simplest and most effective habits you can build.
Annual Tax Check-Ins

Life changes — income changes, family changes, business changes — all have tax implications. An annual tax check-in with a professional (not just at filing time) allows you to adjust your strategy proactively. title: IRS Debt Forgiveness: Does the IRS Ever Just Write Off Debt?
excerpt: You've probably seen ads promising IRS debt forgiveness. While the concept is real — the IRS does in some circumstances write off or forgive tax debt — it's not as simple or as available to everyone as advertising suggests. Here's an honest look at when and how IRS debt actually…
author: SympleTax Team
metaTitle: IRS Debt Forgiveness: Does the IRS Ever Just Write Off Debt? | SympleTax
metaDescription: You've probably seen ads promising IRS debt forgiveness. While the concept is real — the IRS does in some circumstances write off or forgive tax debt — it's n…

content.text: The Reality of IRS Debt Forgiveness

You've probably seen ads promising IRS debt forgiveness. While the concept is real — the IRS does in some circumstances write off or forgive tax debt — it's not as simple or as available to everyone as advertising suggests. Here's an honest look at when and how IRS debt actually gets forgiven.
Statute of Limitations Expiration (CSED)

The most straightforward form of IRS debt forgiveness is the collection statute expiration. The IRS generally has 10 years from the date of assessment to collect. After that date, the debt legally expires and the IRS writes it off. This happens automatically — but the statute can be extended by various taxpayer actions, and the IRS is aggressive about ensuring it doesn't run out unnecessarily.
Offer in Compromise: Negotiated Forgiveness

An accepted Offer in Compromise results in the IRS forgiving (writing off) the difference between what you owe and what you agreed to pay. If you owe $80,000 and your OIC is accepted for $4,000, the remaining $76,000 is legally discharged. This is real, documented forgiveness — not just a promise.
Bankruptcy Discharge

Qualifying income tax debts can be discharged in bankruptcy — another form of legal forgiveness. The requirements are strict (the 3-2-240 rule) but when met, the forgiveness is complete and permanent. Note that bankruptcy has significant credit and financial consequences that must be carefully weighed.
The Tax Consequences of Forgiveness

An important caveat: when debt is forgiven outside of specific IRS programs or bankruptcy, it's generally treated as taxable income. An Offer in Compromise is an exception — settled tax debts under OIC are not considered additional income to the taxpayer. This makes the OIC particularly powerful compared to other forms of debt settlement. title: How the IRS Finds Unreported Income: A Look Behind the Curtain
excerpt: A common misconception: if you don't report income, the IRS won't know about it. Modern IRS data systems are far more sophisticated than most taxpayers realize. Third-party reporting, data analytics, and information sharing agreements mean unreported income is increasingly…

content.text: The IRS Knows More Than You Think

A common misconception: if you don't report income, the IRS won't know about it. Modern IRS data systems are far more sophisticated than most taxpayers realize. Third-party reporting, data analytics, and information sharing agreements mean unreported income is increasingly difficult to hide — and the consequences of getting caught are severe.
Third-Party Information Reporting

Employers, banks, brokerages, payment platforms, and many other entities report income directly to the IRS. W-2s, 1099s, 1099-Ks from payment processors like Venmo and PayPal, 1099-Bs from stock sales, 1099-Rs from retirement distributions — the IRS receives all of these and cross-references them against your return automatically. Unreported items trigger CP2000 notices and additional tax assessments.
The IRS's Discriminant Function System (DIF)

Every tax return is run through the IRS's Discriminant Function System — a sophisticated algorithm that assigns a score based on the likelihood that an audit will produce additional tax. Returns with unusual patterns — unusually high deductions relative to income, missing income categories common to your profession, or statistical anomalies — receive higher scores and are more likely to be selected for examination.
Cash Businesses and IRS Scrutiny

Cash-intensive businesses — restaurants, bars, hair salons, auto repair shops, and others — face disproportionate IRS scrutiny because cash income is easier to underreport. The IRS uses industry-specific gross profit percentage analyses to identify restaurants or retailers reporting profit margins inconsistent with their industry peers. If your numbers don't match, it raises flags.
What to Do If You Have Unreported Income

The best approach if you have unreported income is to come forward proactively. Voluntarily amending returns or using IRS voluntary disclosure programs results in far better outcomes than being caught. Penalties are lower, criminal prosecution is less likely, and resolution options remain fully open. content.text: How COVID Changed the IRS — And What's Different Now

The COVID-19 pandemic created unprecedented disruption in IRS operations and spawned a wave of tax debt for millions of Americans. Understanding the post-pandemic tax landscape — including programs that are still available and changes that have become permanent — helps you navigate today's IRS environment.
Economic Impact Payments and Tax Issues

Millions of Americans received Economic Impact Payments (stimulus checks) that interacted with their taxes in complex ways. Some people who received payments they weren't entitled to (due to income changes between years) faced repayment obligations. Others who didn't receive payments they qualified for could claim a Recovery Rebate Credit. These interactions created lingering IRS issues for many taxpayers.
The Pause and Restart of IRS Collections

During the pandemic, the IRS paused many collection activities and issued unprecedented penalty relief for 2020 and 2021 returns. That pause is now fully lifted. The IRS resumed enforcement with renewed vigor — and a significant backlog of unpaid accounts. If you received pandemic-era IRS relief and assumed it was permanent, it wasn't. Collections are fully active.
Pandemic Economic Injury and IRS Hardship

The economic disruption of COVID-19 remains relevant to IRS resolution in one important way: documented economic hardship caused by the pandemic continues to qualify as Reasonable Cause for penalty abatement requests, particularly for the 2020 and 2021 tax years. This window of opportunity won't remain open forever — but it's still being granted in appropriate cases.
What's Next: Increased IRS Funding and Enforcement

The Inflation Reduction Act provided the IRS with significant additional funding — much of it earmarked for enforcement. The IRS has stated that additional resources will be focused on wealthy individuals and businesses with complex tax situations, but increased enforcement capacity affects all taxpayer segments. If you have unresolved tax issues, the post-pandemic IRS environment makes professional resolution more important than ever. content.text: Taxes Apply to Everyone Earning in the U.S. U.S. tax law applies to virtually anyone who earns income within the United States — not just citizens. Resident aliens, non-resident aliens, and undocumented individuals all have tax obligations. Many immigrants who've worked hard to build lives here find themselves with tax debt through confusion about the rules, language barriers, or fear of engaging with government agencies.
ITIN Filers and Tax Debt

Individuals who can't get a Social Security Number (including many visa holders and undocumented immigrants) can apply for an Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (ITIN) and file tax returns. ITIN filers are subject to the same tax obligations and the same IRS collection activity as SSN holders. They are also eligible for most IRS resolution programs.
Unique Tax Challenges for Immigrants

Immigrants face unique challenges including complex residency rules that determine whether worldwide income is taxable, foreign bank account reporting requirements (FBAR/FATCA), tax treaty implications for certain visa categories, and language barriers that make understanding IRS notices difficult.

items: Dual-status tax years (year of immigration or departure)
Foreign income exclusions and credits for those living partially abroad
Social Security tax exemptions for certain visa categories (F-1, J-1, etc.)
Treaty-based return positions that must be properly claimed

content.text: Immigration Status and IRS Enforcement

The IRS does not share immigration enforcement information with immigration authorities as a standard practice — the IRS is focused on tax collection, not immigration status. Getting compliant with tax obligations is generally advisable regardless of immigration status, as tax compliance can be relevant to immigration proceedings in various ways.

title: How to Handle an IRS Phone Call: What to Say (and What Never to Say)

metaDescription: The IRS calls millions of taxpayers every year — and those calls can be terrifying even when you know you've done nothing wrong. Whether you've initiated the …

content.text: The Call You've Been Dreading

The IRS calls millions of taxpayers every year — and those calls can be terrifying even when you know you've done nothing wrong. Whether you've initiated the call or the IRS has reached out to you, knowing how to handle the conversation can protect your rights and your financial interests.
First: Verify the Call Is Legitimate

IRS phone scams are rampant. The real IRS will never demand immediate payment by gift card, wire transfer, or cryptocurrency; threaten immediate arrest; call without first mailing a notice; or demand payment without giving you an opportunity to question or appeal. If the call feels threatening and urgent in unusual ways, hang up and call the IRS directly at 1-800-829-1040 to verify.
What to Do If It's a Real IRS Call

Get the agent's name, ID number, and callback number. Ask why they're calling and what specifically they need. Do not answer questions you're not prepared for — it's completely acceptable to say 'I'd like to review my records before responding' or 'I'd like to speak with my tax representative first.' Write down everything that's discussed.

items: Ask for the agent's name and employee ID
Get a callback number and the office they're calling from
Take notes on every statement made
Never agree to any resolution on a first call without professional review

content.text: Things You Should Never Say to an IRS Agent

Never volunteer information beyond what's directly asked. Never admit to intentional tax evasion or fraud (even jokingly). Never claim assets or income you then can't document. Never agree to a payment amount in the heat of the moment — the IRS will hold you to it.
The Solution: Have a Professional Take the Calls

content.text: Payroll Taxes: The IRS Takes These Very Seriously

If you employ people, you have payroll tax obligations that the IRS treats with zero tolerance. Employment tax deposits — withheld income tax, Social Security, and Medicare contributions from employees plus the employer's share — must be remitted on a strict schedule. Falling behind even slightly creates a rapidly escalating problem.
The Deposit Schedule: Daily, Semi-Weekly, or Monthly

Your deposit schedule depends on the total payroll tax liability you reported in a lookback period. Small employers may deposit monthly; larger employers must deposit semi-weekly or even daily. Getting this schedule wrong results in failure-to-deposit penalties that begin immediately and can reach 15% of the unpaid amount within days.
The Trust Fund: Not Your Money

The amounts withheld from employees' paychecks — for federal income tax, Social Security employee share, and Medicare employee share — are called 'trust fund taxes.' They're called that because you're holding them in trust for the U.S. government. The IRS views using these funds for business operations (even temporarily) with extreme seriousness. It can and does result in personal liability for business owners through the Trust Fund Recovery Penalty.
Quarterly Form 941 Filing

Form 941 (Employer's Quarterly Federal Tax Return) must be filed every quarter, even if you have no employees in a given quarter (in which case, you may be eligible to file the annual Form 944 instead with IRS permission). Late 941 filings generate immediate penalties on top of any unpaid employment taxes.
If You've Already Fallen Behind on Payroll Taxes

Getting current on payroll taxes while running a business is one of the most complex IRS situations that exists. You may need to address both business entity liability and personal Trust Fund Recovery Penalty liability simultaneously. SympleTax has specific expertise in business employment tax resolution — including strategies to protect business owners from personal liability while resolving the business's obligations. title: IRS Hardship Programs: A Deep Dive Into Economic Hardship Relief

metaDescription: The IRS isn't solely a collection machine — it has programs designed to acknowledge genuine economic hardship and provide relief accordingly. Understanding th…

content.text: When the IRS Acknowledges You Can't Pay

The IRS isn't solely a collection machine — it has programs designed to acknowledge genuine economic hardship and provide relief accordingly. Understanding the full range of hardship-based options, and how to qualify for them, is essential for taxpayers in genuine financial distress.
Currently Not Collectible: Hardship's Most Direct Expression

Currently Not Collectible (CNC) status is the IRS's formal recognition that collecting from you right now would create economic hardship. When CNC is in effect, the IRS suspends all collection activity — no calls, no letters demanding payment, no levies, no garnishments. It's a genuine pause button.

items: How to qualify: allowable expenses must equal or exceed income
Review periods: IRS checks back annually if income sources suggest possible change
Interest and penalties continue to accrue (but no collection)
CSED clock continues running — potentially very beneficial long-term

content.text: Hardship-Based Installment Agreements

If you have some ability to pay but not the amount required under standard installment agreement calculations, you may qualify for a reduced payment installment agreement based on demonstrated hardship. This requires submitting Form 433-A with full financial documentation and is subject to the IRS's review and approval.
Effective Tax Administration OIC

A lesser-known form of the Offer in Compromise is the 'Effective Tax Administration' (ETA) basis — available even if you could technically pay the full amount, but doing so would create economic hardship or be 'unfair and inequitable' given your exceptional circumstances. This basis is used less frequently but can be powerful in the right situation.
Disaster, Death, and Catastrophe

Beyond financial hardship from income limitation, the IRS recognizes specific catastrophic life events as grounds for relief. Death of a spouse or dependent, serious illness or injury, natural disaster, and job loss are all documented hardship circumstances that can support penalty abatement, installment agreement modification, and OIC consideration under the hardship framework. content.text: When Job Loss Creates Tax Problems

Losing your job is already one of life's most stressful events. When it creates or exacerbates IRS tax debt — through underpaid taxes during the year, inability to make installment agreement payments, or the realization that taxes were never fully paid while employed — the combination of stressors can feel overwhelming. There are real solutions available.
Unemployment Benefits Are Taxable

Many people don't realize that unemployment compensation is taxable federal income. Federal income taxes are not automatically withheld from unemployment benefits (you must specifically elect to have 10% withheld). If you received unemployment and didn't elect withholding, you may owe taxes you weren't expecting.
Modifying Existing Installment Agreements After Job Loss

If you already had an IRS installment agreement when you lost your job, you don't have to default. You can call the IRS (or have SympleTax call on your behalf) to modify the agreement based on changed financial circumstances. The IRS can temporarily reduce or suspend payments while you're unemployed. This prevents default and keeps your resolution on track.
Job Loss as Qualifying Hardship for New Applications

If you didn't have a resolution in place and lost your job while owing taxes, your unemployment situation may qualify you for Currently Not Collectible status (if expenses equal or exceed unemployment income), reduced installment agreement payments based on current income, and potentially an Offer in Compromise based on your reduced income projections.
Planning for Re-Employment Tax Complications

When you return to work, it's important to ensure proper withholding from your new job to avoid recreating the tax underpayment problem. This is especially important if you're returning to self-employment or gig work. SympleTax can help you set up a withholding and estimated payment plan as part of your resolution to keep you compliant going forward. content.text: The Unique Tax Situation of Clergy

Ministers, pastors, priests, rabbis, imams, and other religious workers operate under a unique and often misunderstood set of tax rules. Clergy tax situations involve a combination of employee and self-employment treatment that creates complexity — and when handled incorrectly, can result in significant tax debt.
Dual Tax Status: Employed Yet Self-Employed

For most purposes, clergy are treated as employees of their church for federal income tax purposes. However, for Social Security and Medicare (FICA/SECA) purposes, clergy are treated as self-employed — meaning they pay the full 15.3% self-employment tax themselves rather than splitting it with an employer. This surprises many clergy members who don't realize they owe this additional tax.
The Housing Allowance: A Major Benefit Often Misused

The ministerial housing allowance is one of the most significant tax benefits available to clergy — it allows the value of a home or housing allowance provided by the church to be excluded from gross income for federal income tax purposes. However, it does NOT exclude the housing allowance from self-employment tax. Misunderstanding this distinction leads to both underpayment and overpayment errors.
Estimated Tax Payments for Clergy

Since no FICA is withheld from clergy paychecks (even when receiving a W-2 from the church), clergy must pay quarterly estimated taxes to cover their self-employment tax. Many new ministers, unaware of this obligation, accumulate significant payroll tax debt before anyone explains the system to them.
SympleTax Serves Religious Communities

SympleTax understands the unique tax situation of clergy and religious workers. We've helped pastors, ministers, and other faith community leaders resolve tax debt that accumulated through misunderstanding the dual tax status, housing allowance rules, or quarterly payment requirements. Resolution is available, and we approach every client — including those in religious service — with dignity and respect. content.text: The IRS Pays for Information

If you know of significant tax fraud — whether by an employer, business partner, or anyone else — the IRS has a formal Whistleblower Program that pays awards to individuals who report tax fraud that leads to IRS collection. Awards can be substantial, and the program has paid out hundreds of millions of dollars to informants over the years.
How the IRS Whistleblower Program Works

You submit information about suspected tax fraud using Form 211 (Application for Award for Original Information). The IRS reviews the claim and, if they collect taxes, interest, or penalties as a result of your information, you are entitled to 15-30% of the amount collected (for cases involving over $2 million in dispute). For smaller cases, a discretionary award of up to 15% may be available.
What Qualifies as a Reportable Violation

Reportable violations include unreported income, fraudulent deductions, hidden assets offshore, fraudulent payroll schemes, and any other substantial underpayment of federal taxes. The IRS is most interested in high-value cases — individual taxpayers or businesses with over $2 million in underreported taxes generate the largest awards.
Whistleblower Protections

Federal law provides anti-retaliation protections for whistleblowers in certain contexts — particularly employees who report employer tax fraud. If you've faced employment retaliation for reporting tax fraud, you may have legal remedies. The IRS also takes steps to protect whistleblower identity, though complete anonymity cannot be guaranteed in all circumstances.
A Note for SympleTax Clients

If you're dealing with your own IRS debt and have information about significant third-party tax fraud, there is no conflict in pursuing both resolution of your own situation and submission of a whistleblower claim. These are entirely separate processes. SympleTax focuses on resolving your debt; a separate attorney can assist with whistleblower claims if appropriate. content.text: Can a Future Employer See Your IRS Debt?

The short answer: most employers doing standard background checks cannot see your IRS tax debt directly. Your tax return information is protected by federal privacy law (IRC Section 6103). However, the issue is more nuanced than a simple yes or no — and for certain types of positions, tax debt can be a significant employment issue.
Federal Tax Liens and Public Records

While the IRS itself won't share your tax information, federal tax liens are public record. They're filed with local county recorders' offices and can appear in comprehensive background searches, particularly those conducted for financial industry positions or government employment.
Security Clearances and Tax Debt

This is where tax debt becomes most consequential for employment. Security clearance adjudications weigh financial responsibility heavily. Unresolved tax debt — especially when combined with dishonesty or failure to file — can be grounds for denial or revocation of a security clearance under the Adjudicative Guidelines. The government views tax debt as a potential vulnerability to bribery or foreign influence.
Financial Industry Positions

FINRA (Financial Industry Regulatory Authority) and state financial regulators conduct thorough background checks on applicants for securities licenses and financial advisor positions. While tax debt alone doesn't automatically disqualify you, patterns of financial irresponsibility and unresolved tax debt can raise significant flags.
Resolving Debt Before It Affects Your Career

The proactive approach is to resolve tax debt before it becomes a career obstacle. title: The Difference Between Tax Avoidance and Tax Evasion: Know the Line
excerpt: Tax avoidance is legal. Tax evasion is a federal crime. Understanding the difference is not just academic — it determines whether you receive a bill from the IRS or a knock on the door from a federal agent. The line between the two is often misunderstood, and some…
author: SympleTax Team
metaTitle: The Difference Between Tax Avoidance and Tax Evasion: Know the Line | SympleTax
metaDescription: Tax avoidance is legal. Tax evasion is a federal crime. Understanding the difference is not just academic — it determines whether you receive a bill from the …

content.text: Two Very Different Things

Tax avoidance is legal. Tax evasion is a federal crime. Understanding the difference is not just academic — it determines whether you receive a bill from the IRS or a knock on the door from a federal agent. The line between the two is often misunderstood, and some well-intentioned taxpayers inadvertently cross it.
What Is Tax Avoidance?

Tax avoidance is the legal use of tax laws to reduce your tax liability. Every deduction you take, every credit you claim, every retirement contribution you make to defer income — these are all forms of tax avoidance. The tax code is full of legal provisions designed to encourage specific behaviors (homeownership, retirement savings, charitable giving) through tax incentives. Using them is not only legal but intended.
What Is Tax Evasion?

Tax evasion involves an intentional, illegal act — concealing income, falsifying documents, making false statements to the IRS, hiding money in unreported offshore accounts, or deliberately failing to file. The key element is willfulness. Tax evasion is a federal felony punishable by up to 5 years in prison and substantial fines, in addition to the unpaid taxes, penalties, and interest.

items: Underreporting cash income deliberately
Maintaining offshore accounts to hide assets from the IRS
Claiming false deductions with fabricated receipts
Filing fraudulent returns with knowingly incorrect information

content.text: The Gray Zone: Aggressive Tax Positions

Between clearly legal tax planning and outright fraud lies a gray zone of aggressive tax positions — deductions or strategies that may be technically arguable but that the IRS is likely to challenge. Charitable deduction overvaluation, abusive tax shelters, and excessive business deductions relative to income fall into this category. These aren't criminal, but they carry real audit and assessment risk.
When You've Made an Honest Mistake

Many taxpayers with significant IRS debt got there through errors and misunderstanding — not criminal intent. The IRS distinguishes between negligence (making mistakes) and fraud (intentional deception). If you have unreported income or incorrect returns due to honest mistakes or ignorance, getting compliant proactively through amended returns and voluntary disclosure is the correct approach. content.text: When the IRS Goes After Your Business

An IRS levy on a business bank account is one of the most immediately devastating events a business owner can experience. Unlike a personal bank account, your business account may contain payroll funds, vendor payments due, and operating capital — all of which can be frozen in a single IRS action. Speed is absolutely critical.
How Business Account Levies Happen

Business account levies follow the same procedural path as personal levies — final notice, 30-day opportunity to respond — but the consequences unfold faster and more broadly. The IRS will levy any account associated with your Employer Identification Number (EIN). That means checking, savings, and even merchant processing accounts can be frozen simultaneously.
The Payroll Funds Emergency

If your business account is levied and payroll is due, you have a genuine emergency. The IRS has specific procedures for protecting payroll funds when a levy threatens employees' ability to receive wages. Your tax professional can invoke these procedures — but only if you act immediately. SympleTax has experience making emergency calls to the IRS to protect payroll funds in active levy situations.
Business Continuity Arguments

The IRS is not supposed to use collection actions that would prevent a viable business from continuing to operate. If an IRS levy would force you to close, lay off employees, or default on critical obligations, these business continuity arguments can support an expedited release or suspension of levy action. They must be presented compellingly and professionally.
Prevention: The Only Good Strategy

Every business owner who has experienced an IRS levy says the same thing afterward: they wish they had acted sooner. The window between receiving a Final Notice of Intent to Levy and the IRS actually levying your account can be as short as 30 days. If you are a business owner with unresolved IRS debt, SympleTax urges you to act today — before a levy threatens everything you've built. title: What Happens to Tax Debt in Chapter 13 Bankruptcy?
excerpt: While Chapter 7 bankruptcy can discharge qualifying income tax debts, Chapter 13 takes a different approach. In Chapter 13, you propose a 3-5 year repayment plan to pay back creditors — including the IRS — according to a structured schedule approved by the bankruptcy court. For…

metaDescription: While Chapter 7 bankruptcy can discharge qualifying income tax debts, Chapter 13 takes a different approach. In Chapter 13, you propose a 3-5 year repayment p…

content.text: Chapter 13: A Structured Solution for Non-Dischargeable Tax Debt

While Chapter 7 bankruptcy can discharge qualifying income tax debts, Chapter 13 takes a different approach. In Chapter 13, you propose a 3-5 year repayment plan to pay back creditors — including the IRS — according to a structured schedule approved by the bankruptcy court. For tax debts that don't qualify for discharge, Chapter 13 can provide breathing room and structure.
How Tax Debt Is Treated in Chapter 13

In Chapter 13, tax debts are categorized as either priority claims or general unsecured claims. Priority tax claims (recently assessed income taxes, payroll taxes, fraud penalties) must be paid in full through the plan. Non-priority tax claims (older income taxes that meet the 3-2-240 discharge rules) can be discharged at the end of the plan like credit card debt.

items: Priority taxes: paid 100% through the plan
Non-priority taxes: may be paid pennies on the dollar or discharged
Interest stops accruing on priority taxes once the plan is confirmed
Tax liens may persist even after bankruptcy without specific action

content.text: Interest Stoppage: A Significant Benefit

One powerful benefit of Chapter 13 for tax debt is that interest stops accruing on priority tax debts once the bankruptcy plan is confirmed by the court. On a large tax debt where interest accrual is significant, this can save thousands of dollars over the life of the plan.
Automatic Stay: Immediate Protection

Filing Chapter 13 triggers an automatic stay — an immediate halt to all collection actions, including IRS levies, garnishments, and lien enforcement. This immediate protection can be life-saving when the IRS is actively collecting.
Is Chapter 13 Right for You?

Chapter 13 is a powerful tool in specific circumstances — particularly when you have non-dischargeable tax debt combined with other obligations (mortgage arrears, car payments) that you want to reorganize. However, it has significant credit consequences and is complex. SympleTax evaluates whether Chapter 13, IRS resolution programs, or a combination is optimal for each client's unique situation. When facing IRS debt, many taxpayers consider handling their situation themselves. The IRS website has resources, and the prospect of saving professional fees is appealing. DIY resolution seems straightforward — until you're in the middle of it and realize how much you didn't know.
The Hidden Costs of DIY Resolution

The most expensive mistakes in tax resolution are made by taxpayers who didn't know what they didn't know. Agreeing to a higher installment payment than necessary, submitting an Offer in Compromise too early or with the wrong amount, waiving your rights to a Collection Due Process hearing, or triggering actions that toll the statute of limitations — these errors can cost far more than any professional fee.

items: Missed OIC qualification that could have settled for much less
Installment agreement payments above minimum required

Actions that inadvertently extend the IRS collection window by years
Providing information that creates additional IRS scrutiny

IRS agents are not required to tell you about programs that might reduce your liability. They're trained to collect as much as possible. excerpt: The rise of Airbnb, VRBO, and similar platforms has created a new generation of taxpayers with complex and often misunderstood tax obligations. Many hosts are unaware that their rental income creates federal tax liability, state income tax obligations, and in many cases, local…

content.text: The Short-Term Rental Tax Explosion

The rise of Airbnb, VRBO, and similar platforms has created a new generation of taxpayers with complex and often misunderstood tax obligations. Many hosts are unaware that their rental income creates federal tax liability, state income tax obligations, and in many cases, local occupancy or hotel taxes — all of which carry IRS and government enforcement consequences.
How Short-Term Rental Income Is Taxed

Short-term rental income is generally reported on Schedule E (Supplemental Income and Loss). If you provide substantial services to guests (cleaning, meals, concierge), it may instead be reported on Schedule C as business income — which also means self-employment tax. The distinction matters and affects how much you owe.
The 14-Day Rule: A Little-Known Exception

If you rent your home for 14 days or fewer per year, the rental income is completely tax-free — you don't even have to report it. This is the so-called 'master's exemption' sometimes used during major local events (Super Bowl, major golf tournaments). Exceeding 14 days, even by one day, makes all rental income taxable.
1099-K from Airbnb: The IRS Now Knows

Airbnb, VRBO, and similar platforms now issue Form 1099-K to hosts who receive over $600 in payments (threshold as currently legislated — subject to change). The IRS receives a copy automatically. If you've been receiving rental income without reporting it, the IRS almost certainly has a record.
Deductions That Reduce Short-Term Rental Tax

Legitimate deductions for short-term rental hosts include mortgage interest allocated to rental days, property taxes (rental portion), depreciation on the property, cleaning and maintenance, supplies, utilities, platform fees, and a home office if applicable. Proper tracking and allocation of these expenses can significantly reduce your taxable rental income. If you have back taxes from rental income, SympleTax can help reconstruct deductions and develop a resolution strategy. metaDescription: When a loved one leaves you money or property, the last thing you want to think about is the IRS. But certain types of inherited assets do create tax obligati…

content.text: Did You Know Inheritances Can Trigger Tax Bills?

When a loved one leaves you money or property, the last thing you want to think about is the IRS. But certain types of inherited assets do create tax obligations — and being unaware of them can lead to unexpected debt. Here's what triggers inheritance-related taxes and what doesn't.
The Good News: Most Cash Inheritances Aren't Taxable

Cash that you inherit — money left to you directly from an estate, received as a beneficiary of a life insurance policy, or transferred through a bank or investment account — is generally not taxable income to you as the recipient. The estate may owe estate taxes (for very large estates), but you personally don't pay income tax on inherited cash.
Inherited IRAs: The Tax Time Bomb

Inheriting a traditional IRA or 401(k) is a different story. Those funds were never taxed when contributed. When you inherit a traditional retirement account from anyone other than a spouse, you generally must withdraw all funds within 10 years (under the SECURE Act), and every dollar you withdraw is ordinary taxable income. On a $300,000 inherited IRA, the tax bill can be $75,000 or more.
Inherited Property and Step-Up in Basis

When you inherit real estate or stocks, you receive what's called a 'step-up in basis' — your cost basis is adjusted to the fair market value at the date of the decedent's death. This means if you sell the inherited property shortly after inheriting it, you likely owe little or no capital gains tax. This is one of the most powerful tax benefits in the tax code.
Planning Ahead for Inherited Tax Obligations

The best time to plan for the tax consequences of an inheritance is before you receive it — ideally in coordination with the estate planning of the person leaving assets to you. title: How Tax Debt Affects Your Passport and International Travel
excerpt: Since 2018, the IRS has had the authority to certify 'seriously delinquent' tax debt to the State Department, which can then deny, revoke, or refuse to renew your U.S. passport. This authority has been used against tens of thousands of taxpayers — and the threshold for what…

metaDescription: Since 2018, the IRS has had the authority to certify 'seriously delinquent' tax debt to the State Department, which can then deny, revoke, or refuse to renew …

content.text: The IRS Can Ground Your International Travel

Since 2018, the IRS has had the authority to certify 'seriously delinquent' tax debt to the State Department, which can then deny, revoke, or refuse to renew your U.S. passport. This authority has been used against tens of thousands of taxpayers — and the threshold for what counts as 'seriously delinquent' may be lower than you expect.
What Counts as Seriously Delinquent Tax Debt?

For 2024, the IRS can certify your debt to the State Department if you owe more than $59,000 in assessed federal taxes, penalties, and interest, AND the IRS has filed a tax lien or issued a levy against you. The $59,000 threshold is adjusted for inflation annually.
How the Passport Certification Process Works

The IRS certifies eligible accounts to the Treasury Department, which notifies the State Department. If you apply for a new passport or passport renewal, the State Department can deny your application. If you already have a passport, it can be revoked. The IRS must notify you by mail of the certification — though many taxpayers first learn of it when their passport is denied.
Exceptions to Passport Certification

Certain taxpayers are exempt from certification even with delinquent debt — those in an installment agreement, those with an Offer in Compromise pending or accepted, those who have requested a Collection Due Process hearing, those in Currently Not Collectible status, and those in bankruptcy. Entering any of these programs not only resolves your debt — it protects your ability to travel.
Resolving Passport Issues Quickly

If you're facing passport denial or revocation due to tax debt, time is critical — especially if you have international travel planned or a job that requires international travel. content.text: High-Income Professionals Are Not Immune to Tax Debt

Physicians, dentists, and other medical professionals often assume that high income means tax problems are unlikely. In reality, high-income professionals face unique tax vulnerabilities — complex compensation structures, practice ownership, multiple income sources, and high tax rates — that can create significant tax debt when not managed carefully.
Common Causes of Medical Professional Tax Debt

Medical professionals most commonly encounter tax debt through: transitioning from residency to attending physician compensation without adjusting withholding; starting a private practice without properly accounting for self-employment tax and quarterly payments; taking large practice distributions in good years without setting aside adequate tax reserves; and rapid income growth that moves them into higher brackets for which their withholding is inadequate.

items: Inadequate withholding during income transition years
Self-employment tax underestimation for practice owners
Missed quarterly estimated payments
Investment income from hospital or surgery center partnerships not accounted for
Complex K-1 income from professional corporations or partnerships

content.text: Medical Practice Business Tax Issues

Beyond personal tax debt, medical practices themselves face employment tax obligations, potential payroll tax issues, and complex business income reporting. A medical practice with 20 employees that falls behind on payroll tax remittances can accumulate debt very quickly — and the physician-owner may face personal Trust Fund Recovery Penalty liability.
High Income, High Stakes Resolution

IRS resolution for high-income professionals requires sophisticated analysis. Physician income may fluctuate significantly, making accurate financial projection for OIC calculations complex. Asset profiles may be substantial, affecting what the IRS believes can be collected. SympleTax has experience with high-income resolution cases and understands how to navigate these complexities.
Protecting Your Medical License

In some states, unresolved tax debt — particularly tax liens — can affect professional licensing. Medical boards in certain jurisdictions are notified of significant tax delinquencies. Proactive resolution through SympleTax protects not just your finances but your professional standing and license. content.text: Stimulus Payments Created More IRS Issues Than People Realize

The COVID-19 stimulus programs — three rounds of Economic Impact Payments totaling up to $3,200 per adult — were intended to help Americans. But they also created a significant number of IRS complications that continue to generate notices and confusion years later.
The Recovery Rebate Credit: Did You Get What You Were Owed?

If you didn't receive your full stimulus payments (because your income was too high in the year the IRS used to calculate eligibility, but lower in the actual year), you may have been entitled to claim the Recovery Rebate Credit on your tax return. Many Americans missed this credit and left money on the table.
When the IRS Says You Owe Stimulus Money Back

Some taxpayers received more in stimulus payments than they were entitled to — for example, if they received payments on behalf of deceased individuals, or if their 2020/2021 income was higher than the year used for calculation. The IRS has sent notices to these taxpayers seeking repayment. However, the rules on repayment of overpaid stimulus payments are specific, and not all overpayments are actually repayable — it depends on the specific payment round and circumstances.
Stimulus and Identity Theft

A significant number of stimulus fraud cases involved identity theft — someone else claiming stimulus payments using another person's Social Security Number. If you received a notice about stimulus payments you never actually received, identity theft is a possible explanation. The IRS has a specific process for resolving identity theft-related tax issues.
Getting Help With Stimulus-Related IRS Issues

Stimulus-related IRS issues range from simple clarification needed on a notice to complex situations involving multiple years of returns, identity theft complications, and outstanding balances. content.text: Your Divorce Attorney Has One Job — And It's Not IRS Resolution

Divorce attorneys are skilled at navigating family law — asset division, custody, alimony, and property settlement. But tax law is a specialized discipline, and the intersection of divorce and IRS tax debt creates issues that family law attorneys often don't anticipate or fully understand. The results can be costly for their clients.
The Most Common Divorce-Tax Mistakes

Allocating tax debt to one spouse in a decree without understanding that the IRS isn't bound by it; failing to file for Innocent Spouse Relief when it would have been available; incorrect handling of retirement account division (QDRO requirements); misunderstanding the tax treatment of alimony (post-TCJA, alimony is no longer deductible for payer or taxable to recipient for divorces finalized after 2018); and failing to address joint IRS installment agreements in divorce proceedings.

items: Divorce decree doesn't bind the IRS — both spouses remain liable
Innocent Spouse Relief has strict filing deadlines that divorce attorneys often miss
QDRO must be handled correctly or retirement division creates immediate tax liability
Joint installment agreements need to be specifically addressed in divorce

content.text: The Injured Spouse Timing Issue

If you have a joint tax refund coming and you file for divorce while your spouse has outstanding tax debt or student loans, the Treasury Offset Program may seize your portion of the refund. Filing Form 8379 (Injured Spouse Allocation) before or with your joint return is critical — and most divorce attorneys don't think to advise this.
When to Bring in a Tax Professional

Any divorce involving significant assets, joint tax debt, business interests, retirement accounts, or unequal income levels should involve a tax professional alongside the divorce attorney. The tax professional can model the after-tax impact of different settlement structures, ensure the divorce decree addresses tax issues correctly, and advise on IRS-specific protections available to you.

content.text: Disability and Tax Debt: A Frequently Overlooked Issue

Americans with disabilities face unique challenges in managing their tax obligations — and unique opportunities for IRS relief. Whether your disability affects your ability to earn income, manage complex financial matters, or simply respond to IRS correspondence in a timely manner, the IRS has provisions that recognize these realities.
Is Social Security Disability Income Taxable?

SSDI (Social Security Disability Insurance) may be taxable, depending on your total income. If your combined income (SSDI plus other income) exceeds $25,000 for single filers or $32,000 for joint filers, up to 85% of your SSDI may be taxable. Many disability recipients don't realize this and don't have taxes withheld from their payments, creating unexpected tax bills.
SSI Is Not Taxable

Supplemental Security Income (SSI) — the means-tested disability program — is not taxable at all. If your only income is SSI, you have no federal income tax obligation. The confusion between SSDI and SSI leads many disabled individuals to either file unnecessarily or fail to file when they should.
Disability as Reasonable Cause for Penalty Abatement

A documented disability that prevented timely filing or payment is a well-recognized basis for Reasonable Cause penalty abatement. The IRS specifically recognizes serious illness or incapacity as grounds for abatement. Medical documentation from treating physicians strengthens these requests significantly.
Currently Not Collectible for Disabled Taxpayers

Disabled individuals on fixed incomes — particularly those whose allowable living expenses (including medical costs) equal or exceed their disability income — frequently qualify for Currently Not Collectible status. With proper documentation of medical expenses and income limitations, the IRS can suspend all collection activity indefinitely. SympleTax has significant experience helping disabled clients achieve CNC status and other relief. content.text: Partnerships and the IRS: More Complexity Than You'd Think

Business partnerships — whether formal LLPs, general partnerships, or multi-member LLCs taxed as partnerships — create a layer of tax complexity that can lead partners into unexpected tax debt. Understanding how partnership taxation works is essential for any partner.
How Partnership Income Is Taxed

Partnerships don't pay federal income tax themselves — they're 'pass-through' entities. Income, losses, deductions, and credits pass through to partners proportionally, reported to each partner on a Schedule K-1. Each partner pays tax on their share of partnership income whether or not they actually received a cash distribution. Partners who didn't receive cash distributions can find themselves owing taxes on 'phantom income.'
Phantom Income: Owing Taxes on Money You Didn't Receive

If the partnership had $500,000 in taxable income but reinvested it all rather than distributing cash to partners, each partner still owes tax on their pro-rata share. A 30% partner owes taxes on $150,000 of income they may never have seen. This is one of the most common ways partners accumulate tax debt — and it's particularly brutal in early years of real estate and investment partnerships.
Partnership Audit Rules: The BBA

The Bipartisan Budget Act (BBA) of 2015 changed partnership audit rules significantly. The IRS now generally assesses and collects tax at the partnership level (not from individual partners) in partnership audits. However, partnerships can elect out of these rules (if they have 100 or fewer qualifying partners), and understanding the implications for individual partners requires expert analysis.
Resolving Partnership-Related Tax Debt

Partners who owe personal tax on partnership income have access to all standard IRS resolution programs. The key is ensuring the financial analysis properly accounts for the structure of the debt — distinguishing between phantom income years and actual cash distribution years, and correctly representing the partner's current financial picture. content.text: Resolution Is the Beginning, Not the End

When your IRS tax debt is finally resolved — whether through an Offer in Compromise, installment agreement completion, or another program — it's a moment worth celebrating. But it's also the beginning of a new chapter. Rebuilding your finances after tax resolution requires intentional steps to ensure you never find yourself in the same position again.
Getting Your Credit Back on Track

Tax resolution affects credit in several ways. Once an Offer in Compromise is completed or an installment agreement is paid off, the IRS releases any tax liens within 30 days. Confirm that the lien release is properly recorded with the county recorder. If the lien appeared in public records searches (even if not on your credit report), confirming its removal is important for financial applications.
Building an Emergency Tax Fund

One of the leading causes of tax debt is having no cash reserve when taxes come due. Establishing a dedicated tax savings account — contributing a fixed percentage of every paycheck or revenue receipt — prevents future tax debt from forming. For the self-employed, 25-30% of net income; for W-2 employees, ensure withholding covers your expected liability with a small buffer.
Establishing Proper Bookkeeping

Many clients come to SympleTax because they lacked basic financial organization — they couldn't track income, couldn't document deductions, and couldn't reconstruct records when the IRS came calling. Post-resolution is the perfect time to implement proper bookkeeping, whether through accounting software, a part-time bookkeeper, or a full-service accounting firm.
Annual Tax Check-Ins Going Forward

The IRS situation you just resolved probably didn't develop overnight — it grew over months or years. An annual check-in with a tax professional (not just a tax preparer at filing time, but a proactive advisor) ensures small issues get addressed before they become large ones. content.text: The Document That Starts Your Settlement

If you want to settle your IRS tax debt for less than you owe through an Offer in Compromise, you begin with IRS Form 656 — the official application. Understanding what Form 656 requires, what it commits you to, and how to complete it correctly is essential for a successful OIC submission.
What Form 656 Contains

Form 656 is the formal offer document itself — it states the amount you're offering and the terms of payment. It's submitted along with Form 433-A (OIC) (your detailed financial statement), all required supporting documentation, a $205 application fee (waived for low-income applicants below 250% of federal poverty guidelines), and your initial payment (either 20% of the offer amount for lump sum, or the first monthly payment for periodic payment offers).
Two Payment Options

Form 656 offers two payment structures. Lump Sum Cash Offer: you offer to pay the settlement amount within 5 months of acceptance, submitting 20% upfront with the application. Periodic Payment Offer: you propose to pay the settlement in 6-24 monthly installments, submitting the first installment with the application and continuing payments during IRS evaluation.

items: Lump Sum: 20% upfront, balance within 5 months of acceptance — IRS views more favorably
Periodic Payment: monthly payments continue during evaluation (applied to balance if rejected)
Low Income Certification: waives application fee and initial payment requirement

content.text: The OIC Booklet: Read It Carefully

Form 656 is packaged with the OIC Booklet (Form 656-B), which explains the entire program, eligibility requirements, and process. The IRS expects applicants to have reviewed this material thoroughly. Your financial disclosures must be complete, accurate, and consistent with the booklet's requirements.
Why Professional Preparation Is Critical

An OIC application is a legal document submitted under penalty of perjury. Errors, omissions, or inconsistencies can result in automatic rejection, additional IRS scrutiny, or worse. SympleTax prepares and submits all OIC applications with meticulous attention to accuracy, strategic framing, and complete documentation — maximizing acceptance probability.
