content.text: State Tax Debt: Just as Real, Often More Aggressive

While much tax relief discussion focuses on the IRS, state tax agencies collect their own income taxes (where applicable) and have their own enforcement mechanisms. For residents of the most populous states, state tax debt can be just as significant as federal debt — and the resolution process is different for each state.
California: FTB and the Most Aggressive Collection State

California's Franchise Tax Board (FTB) is widely considered one of the most aggressive state tax collectors in the nation. The FTB has broad authority to garnish wages, levy bank accounts, and file liens without the procedural requirements the IRS must follow. California's personal income tax rates are among the highest in the nation (up to 13.3%), making underpayment issues significant. California does offer installment agreements and an Offer in Compromise program, though the OIC program is more restrictive than the federal equivalent.
Texas and Florida: No State Income Tax, But Other State Taxes

Texas and Florida have no state personal income tax — so their residents don't face state income tax debt. However, Texas and Florida both have significant sales tax systems, and Texas has a franchise tax (margin tax) on businesses. Business owners in these states can still accumulate significant state tax debt through sales tax non-compliance or franchise tax issues.
New York: DTF and Its Long Reach

New York's Department of Taxation and Finance (DTF) is known for aggressive multi-state enforcement. New York will pursue tax from non-residents who earn New York-sourced income, and the state has a 'statutory residency' rule that can make someone a New York taxpayer even if they consider themselves a resident of another state. New York offers installment plans and has a formal offer in compromise program.
SympleTax Handles State and Federal Debt Together

Clients often come to SympleTax with both federal and state tax debt. Managing resolution across multiple agencies simultaneously requires careful coordination — the resolution of federal debt affects your financial picture for state purposes and vice versa. SympleTax handles multi-state and federal-state resolution comprehensively, ensuring you don't resolve one problem only to have another explode. content.text: Why People Wait — Even When They Know They Shouldn't

Ask anyone who has accumulated significant IRS debt and they'll tell you: they knew they needed to act earlier. They received the notices. They understood it was getting worse. But something kept them from picking up the phone. Understanding the psychology of tax procrastination is the first step to breaking the cycle.
The Shame-Avoidance Loop

Many people delay addressing tax debt because it's tied to feelings of shame, failure, or inadequacy. Financial problems feel like personal failures. Opening IRS notices feels like being confronted with evidence of those failures. The avoidance provides temporary relief — until the next notice arrives, larger than the last, and the shame deepens. It's a cycle that compounds both financially and emotionally.
The Illusory Safety of Avoidance

The IRS won't come to your door tomorrow if you avoid the problem today. This creates an illusion of safety — 'nothing bad happened yet.' But every month of avoidance adds interest (approximately 8% annually), failure-to-pay penalties (0.5% per month up to 25%), and potentially failure-to-file penalties (5% per month up to 25%). The financial cost of a year of avoidance on a $50,000 balance can easily exceed $7,000-$10,000.
Breaking the Cycle: Information as the Antidote

The most effective antidote to tax avoidance is information. Most people who finally call SympleTax tell us that the reality was far less terrifying than what they had imagined during years of avoidance. Simply knowing your actual balance, your actual options, and a realistic path forward removes the fog of dread and replaces it with a manageable plan.
The Cost of Every Day You Wait

We don't say this to increase anxiety — we say it because it's mathematically true: every day without a resolution costs you money. An Offer in Compromise settled for $5,000 today costs you nothing extra. An OIC submitted six months from now after $3,000 in additional interest and penalties has already accrued gives the IRS less reason to settle. Call today. The best day to start is always today. title: IRS Payment Plan Default: What Happens and How to Recover
excerpt: Entering an IRS installment agreement is a significant step toward resolution — but it's not the finish line. Installment agreements come with conditions, and violating those conditions can cause your agreement to default. When an agreement defaults, the IRS can immediately…

metaDescription: Entering an IRS installment agreement is a significant step toward resolution — but it's not the finish line. Installment agreements come with conditions, and…

content.text: When Your Payment Plan Falls Apart

Entering an IRS installment agreement is a significant step toward resolution — but it's not the finish line. Installment agreements come with conditions, and violating those conditions can cause your agreement to default. When an agreement defaults, the IRS can immediately resume collection activity — including wage garnishments and bank levies.
What Causes an Installment Agreement to Default

The most common reasons IRS installment agreements default include: missing or late monthly payments, failing to file required tax returns for subsequent years, incurring a new tax liability that's not paid promptly, providing false or inaccurate financial information on your application, and failing to update the IRS about significant changes in your financial circumstances.

items: Missed payment (even one can trigger default)
Unfiled return for any subsequent tax year
New balance due not addressed within 30 days of filing
Change of address not reported to IRS (missed correspondence = missed deadlines)

content.text: CP523: The Default Notice

When the IRS believes your installment agreement has defaulted, they send Notice CP523 — Notice of Intent to Terminate Your Installment Agreement. You have 30 days from this notice to either cure the default (make the missed payment, file the missing return, etc.) or request a resolution before the agreement is formally terminated.
Reinstating a Defaulted Agreement

In many cases, a defaulted agreement can be reinstated. If the default was a single missed payment or an inadvertent filing failure, the IRS will often reinstate the agreement after you cure the default and explain the circumstances. However, repeated defaults or willful non-compliance make reinstatement more difficult.
Using Default as an Opportunity to Reassess

A defaulted installment agreement is a signal that the terms weren't workable — and it's an opportunity to revisit your resolution strategy. If your financial situation has changed significantly since the agreement was set up, you may now qualify for an Offer in Compromise, a lower payment amount, or Currently Not Collectible status. SympleTax helps clients recover from agreement defaults and find a more sustainable path forward. title: IRS Statute of Limitations on Refunds: Are You Leaving Money Behind?
excerpt: The IRS has a statute of limitations on collecting taxes — but there's an equally important deadline that works in your favor: the statute of limitations on claiming a tax refund. If you overpaid taxes or are owed a refund, you generally have three years from the original due…

content.text: The Three-Year Rule Most Taxpayers Don't Know

The IRS has a statute of limitations on collecting taxes — but there's an equally important deadline that works in your favor: the statute of limitations on claiming a tax refund. If you overpaid taxes or are owed a refund, you generally have three years from the original due date of the return to claim it. After that, the money is forfeited to the Treasury.
How Much Money Is Left Unclaimed?

The IRS holds hundreds of millions of dollars each year in unclaimed refunds from unfiled returns. People who didn't file because they were below the filing threshold, were confused about their obligation, or simply didn't realize they had a refund coming — all of these individuals lose their refund after the three-year window closes. The IRS has no obligation to notify you.
When the Three-Year Clock Starts

The three-year period runs from the original due date of the return (April 15 of the following year), not from when you actually file. If you filed an extension, the clock may run from the extension deadline instead. If you file a return more than three years after its due date, you may still owe any taxes shown — but you cannot receive a refund.
Amended Returns and Refund Claims

If you filed a return and later realize you overpaid — through missed deductions, incorrect reporting, or an error — you can file an amended return (Form 1040-X) to claim a refund. The same three-year window applies. Acting promptly on identified over-payments is important.
Reviewing Old Returns for Missed Refunds

SympleTax sometimes discovers, during transcript analysis for tax debt clients, that the client actually had a refund coming for a year they assumed they owed — a refund they would have received if they'd filed correctly and timely. In some cases, those refunds can still be claimed if within the three-year window. This is one of many reasons why comprehensive transcript review at the start of every case pays dividends. title: How the IRS Treats Gambling Winnings and Losses: What Every Player Needs to Know
excerpt: Every dollar you win gambling — at a casino, racetrack, online, through lottery, or in any other form — is taxable income that must be reported on your federal tax return. This includes jackpots, poker tournament winnings, sports betting proceeds, and even non-cash prizes. The…
author: SympleTax Team
metaTitle: How the IRS Treats Gambling Winnings and Losses: What Every Player Needs to Know | SympleTax
metaDescription: Every dollar you win gambling — at a casino, racetrack, online, through lottery, or in any other form — is taxable income that must be reported on your federa…

content.text: Gambling Winnings Are Taxable Income

Every dollar you win gambling — at a casino, racetrack, online, through lottery, or in any other form — is taxable income that must be reported on your federal tax return. This includes jackpots, poker tournament winnings, sports betting proceeds, and even non-cash prizes. The IRS takes gambling income seriously, and casinos are required to report certain winnings directly to the IRS.
W-2G: When Casinos Report to the IRS

Casinos and other gambling establishments issue Form W-2G for certain types of winnings above specific thresholds: $1,200 or more from slots or bingo, $1,500 or more from keno, $5,000 or more from poker tournaments, and any winnings subject to 24% federal withholding. These W-2Gs go directly to the IRS — your winnings are automatically on their radar.
Deducting Gambling Losses

The good news: gambling losses are deductible — but only up to the amount of your gambling winnings, and only if you itemize deductions (not if you take the standard deduction). You cannot net your winnings and losses and report just the net — you must report all winnings as income and separately deduct losses up to that amount. You must also have documentation: casino player's card records, receipts, and a detailed gambling log.
Professional Gamblers: A Different Set of Rules

If gambling is your primary profession (a legal status that requires significant proof), your gambling activity is reported on Schedule C as a business. This means you can deduct gambling-related business expenses (travel to casinos, entry fees, research costs) beyond just losses. However, you also pay self-employment tax on net gambling income — an additional 15.3%.
Online Gambling and Sports Betting: Fully Taxable

The legalization of sports betting across many states has created a new category of gambling income that many bettors treat as informal. It's not. Sports betting winnings are fully taxable. Online platforms now issue 1099-type reporting for significant winnings. If you've won money through sports betting or online gambling and haven't reported it, SympleTax can help you get compliant and resolve any resulting tax liability. content.text: MLM Income Is Self-Employment Income

If you've participated in multi-level marketing (MLM) or direct sales organizations — companies like Amway, Herbalife, LuLaRoe, or hundreds of others — your commission and bonus income is self-employment income. This means it's subject to both regular income tax and self-employment tax of 15.3%. Many distributors receive 1099s from their MLM company and are surprised to discover their tax bill at year end.
The Inventory and Business Expense Reality

Many MLM distributors purchase significant inventory (required or pressured by the company structure) without being able to resell it profitably. These inventory costs, along with legitimate business expenses like home office, vehicle use for deliveries, promotional materials, and training events, may be deductible against MLM income. Proper documentation of these expenses reduces taxable income significantly.
Hobby Loss Rules and MLM

The IRS applies hobby loss rules to activities that don't show a profit in at least 3 of 5 years. If your MLM activity consistently loses money (as the majority do, according to income disclosure statements), the IRS may reclassify it as a hobby — which means your MLM expenses are no longer deductible at all against other income, while any income remains taxable. This creates a painful asymmetry.
Common Tax Debt Scenarios for MLM Participants

The most common path to tax debt for MLM distributors: receiving significant 1099 income during a 'good year,' not setting aside estimated taxes, the year's income evaporating as the business stalls, and then owing taxes on money long since spent. This pattern is extremely common, and the resolution programs available to self-employed individuals apply fully to MLM distributors.
Getting Help With MLM-Related Tax Issues

Whether you're currently active in a network marketing business or you've since moved on and left behind a tax debt from those years, SympleTax can help. We understand the specific deduction landscape, can review whether hobby loss reclassification applies, and can develop a resolution strategy that addresses the unique characteristics of MLM-related income and expense. content.text: The Rules Changed in 2018 — And Many People Still Don't Know

The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017 fundamentally changed how alimony is taxed — and the change caught many divorced and divorcing individuals completely off guard. If your divorce was finalized after December 31, 2018, the alimony tax rules that applied to your parents' generation no longer apply to you.
The Old Rules (Pre-2019 Divorces)

Under the old rules, alimony was deductible by the paying spouse (above the line, reducing adjusted gross income) and taxable income to the receiving spouse. This created a tax efficiency where higher-income payers in higher brackets paid less in after-tax costs by making payments that were taxable to lower-bracket recipients.
The New Rules (Post-2018 Divorces)

For divorce agreements executed after December 31, 2018: alimony is NOT deductible by the payer, and alimony is NOT taxable income to the recipient. This is a complete reversal. The practical effect is that alimony costs the payer significantly more in after-tax dollars, because there's no offsetting deduction. Many divorce settlements negotiated after 2018 need to account for this change in the overall economic analysis.
Modifications to Pre-2019 Agreements

If you modify a pre-2019 divorce agreement, the new rules apply only if the modification specifically states that the post-2018 rules will apply. A simple modification for other purposes (custody, for example) does not automatically change the tax treatment. This nuance creates potential pitfalls and requires careful language in modification agreements.
Tax Debt From Alimony Misclassification

Spouses who deducted alimony payments from post-2018 divorces have received IRS notices disallowing the deduction and assessing additional taxes. Spouses who received alimony from pre-2019 divorces and failed to report it as income have also faced IRS assessments. If you've received an IRS notice related to alimony treatment, SympleTax can review the specific facts of your divorce agreement and determine the correct tax treatment — and resolve any resulting liability. content.text: Compliance Is Freedom

At SympleTax, we've seen what tax debt does to people — the stress, the fear, the financial damage, the impact on families and relationships. We've also seen the transformation that happens when it's resolved. Our deepest goal is that every client we serve never has to come back to us for the same reason. This guide is our comprehensive gift to you: everything you need to stay compliant and tax-problem-free for life.
The Five Pillars of Lifelong Tax Compliance
Based on years of working with taxpayers who found themselves in trouble, we've identified five core habits that prevent virtually every common tax problem:

items: File every required return — even if you can't pay
Pay as you go — withholding or quarterly estimates
Keep organized records year-round, not just at tax time
Respond to every IRS notice immediately, never ignore them
Work with a tax professional proactively, not just reactively

content.text: The File-Don't-Pay Principle

The single most important compliance habit: always file your return, even if you can't pay what you owe. The failure-to-file penalty (5% per month up to 25%) is ten times larger than the failure-to-pay penalty (0.5% per month up to 25%). Filing with no payment saves you thousands in penalties and keeps your resolution options wide open.
Building a Tax Calendar

Create a tax calendar with these annual milestones: January (gather W-2s, 1099s; review last year's withholding), February-March (prepare or gather documents for tax preparer), April 15 (file or extend; pay any balance; Q1 estimated payment due), June 15 (Q2 estimated payment), September 15 (Q3 estimated payment), October 15 (extended return due), January 15 (Q4 estimated payment).
When to Call SympleTax Going Forward

Even after your current situation is resolved, there are times when calling SympleTax is the right move: you receive any IRS notice you don't fully understand; your income changes significantly (up or down); you start or end a business; you go through a major life event (marriage, divorce, death of spouse); you receive a large unexpected payment or inheritance; or you're simply uncertain about your tax obligations. We're here for all of it — not just crisis management, but proactive partnership in your financial health.
